target.title: fix: preserve web tools across stripped secrets refresh
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #77826. A stripped runtime config write can take the secrets runtime fast path and produce empty web-tool metadata. Activating that snapshot used to overwrite the still-valid web search/fetch metadata from the previous full resolver pass, so web tools could disappear after a config write even though the real config still owned them.

## Why This Change Was Made

Prepared secrets snapshots now carry explicit provenance for whether web-tool metadata came from the fast path. During activation, a stripped fast-path snapshot may preserve populated active web-tool metadata only when the previous full resolver source config contained a web-tool metadata container. Full resolver snapshots and explicit empty `tools.web` / `plugins.entries` containers remain authoritative clears.

The duplicate preflight checked exact issue-number PR searches and root-cause keywords. I found only closed or superseded work (#77859, #78067, #78268, #82562) and no active PR owning #77826.

## User Impact

Users keep configured web search/fetch tools after config writes that omit unrelated web-tool surfaces. Explicitly clearing web/plugin containers still removes the active metadata, so this does not turn stale provider credentials into a hidden fallback.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/secrets/runtime.fast-path.test.ts src/secrets/runtime-web-tools-state.test.ts src/config/runtime-snapshot.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Structured autoreview was attempted before this draft, but the Codex app rejected the helper because it would export repository diffs to an external review/model service under the current workspace policy. I did not route around that policy.

AI assistance disclosure: this draft PR was prepared with Codex assistance. No redacted agent transcript is included because the transcript workflow requires explicit user approval before adding local session logs to a PR body.


---

**Runtime proof — captured before/after (negative-control harness, Node 22):**

Beyond the colocated unit tests, the fix was exercised through the REAL secrets-runtime state machine — `prepareSecretsRuntimeSnapshot` (resolver + fast path), `activateSecretsRuntimeSnapshot`, and the **real config-write refresh handler** `finalizeRuntimeSnapshotWrite` (`src/config/runtime-snapshot.ts:294`, the exact path the gateway invokes on every config write). This is an internal-state bug, so there is no external boundary to mock — the harness controls only config inputs and reads back `getActiveRuntimeWebToolsMetadata()`. Same harness, run on `main` and on this branch.

_Before — unfixed `main` (exit 1):_

```
Scenario 1  stripped fast-path refresh must PRESERVE active web tools
  active BEFORE refresh: search.selectedProvider="brave" providerSource="configured"
  active AFTER  refresh: search.selectedProvider=undefined providerSource="none"
  [FAIL] stripped refresh did NOT preserve active web_search  (the #77826 bug)
RESULT: FAIL — #77826 bug signature.
```

_After — this branch (exit 0):_

```
Scenario 1 (stripped refresh)                  active AFTER: selectedProvider="brave"     [PASS] preserved
Scenario 2 (canonical web-config deletion, P1) active AFTER: selectedProvider=undefined   [PASS] cleared
Scenario 3 (stripped refresh + plugin entry)   active AFTER: selectedProvider="brave"     [PASS] preserved
RESULT: PASS — stripped refreshes preserve active web tools; canonical deletion still clears.
```

Same harness + inputs: Scenarios 1 & 3 flip from `undefined`/`"none"` (clobbered) on `main` to `"brave"` (preserved) on this branch, while Scenario 2 (the P1 deletion-clears) holds on both. Supporting colocated tests (`runtime-web-tools-state.test.ts`, `runtime.fast-path.test.ts`): 16 passed.

**What was not tested:** no live external web-search/fetch HTTP call (the defect is purely internal snapshot-activation state); the config-write refresh is driven via the real `finalizeRuntimeSnapshotWrite` handler rather than a full live gateway hot-reload (same handler the gateway uses).

target.title: fix: block no-auth managed gateway LAN installs
target.body:
Related: #97970

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users with a managed Gateway service could end up with a dead Dashboard after the service is refreshed or restarted while `gateway.bind` resolves outside loopback and `gateway.auth.mode` is explicitly `none`.

I hit this on a real Windows Scheduled Task install after a Dashboard `/pair qr` attempt. The Gateway service was stopped, a manual gateway start refused to bind, and the Dashboard stopped responding. The redacted config shape was:

```text
gateway.bind = lan
gateway.auth.mode = none
gateway.auth.token = configured
```

The runtime fail-closed message was:

```text
Refusing to bind gateway to lan without auth.
```

## Why This Change Was Made

The existing Gateway runtime safety guard is correct: an unauthenticated Gateway must not silently bind to LAN. This change moves the same safety check earlier into the managed install/refresh path so `openclaw gateway install --force` stops before writing or refreshing a service that the runtime will reject.

The install guard resolves the effective bind host with the Gateway bind resolver, resolves the effective auth mode from the install environment, and blocks only the invalid non-loopback + explicit no-auth combination. Loopback no-auth installs, token/password auth, trusted-proxy auth, and existing token generation paths remain on the existing flow.

## User Impact

Operators get an actionable install-time error instead of a Gateway service that exits or restart-loops and leaves the Dashboard unreachable. When a token is already configured but inactive because `auth.mode` is `none`, the error points users at `openclaw config set gateway.auth.mode token` before rerunning `openclaw gateway install --force`.

## Evidence

Real Windows incident proof, redacted:

```text
OpenClaw 2026.6.11-beta.2
Gateway service: Windows Scheduled Task
Observed config: gateway.bind=lan, gateway.auth.mode=none, gateway.auth.token present
Manual gateway start: Refusing to bind gateway to lan without auth.
After local config repair: gateway.auth.mode=token
openclaw gateway status: running, connectivity probe ok, auth token, listening 0.0.0.0:18889
openclaw status: Gateway reachable, auth token, Gateway service running
```

After-fix managed install proof on this branch, using an isolated `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` with `gateway.bind=lan`, `gateway.auth.mode=none`, and a redacted configured token:

```text
$ node scripts\run-node.mjs gateway install --force --json
{
  "action": "install",
  "ok": false,
  "error": "Gateway install blocked: gateway.bind=lan resolves to 0.0.0.0, but gateway.auth.mode=none disables Gateway auth. This config already has gateway.auth.token; run openclaw config set gateway.auth.mode token and then rerun openclaw gateway install --force."
}
exit=1
```

This proves the current branch stops the managed install/refresh path before service rewrite for the invalid no-auth LAN configuration.

Local validation on this branch:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli/install.test.ts -- --reporter dot --pool threads --maxWorkers 1 --no-file-parallelism
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       25 passed (25)

pnpm tsgo:core --pretty false
passed

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cli/daemon-cli/install.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/install.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
passed

python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
```

Proof limitation: I did not prove the broader claim from #97970 that `openclaw update` itself writes `gateway.bind=lan` when the field was previously omitted. This PR intentionally targets the confirmed managed install/refresh boundary and preserves the runtime no-auth LAN refusal.

target.title: fix(claude-cli): surface re-auth hint when subprocess OAuth token expires
target.body:
## Summary

- Surfaces a targeted Claude CLI re-auth hint when the subprocess OAuth token expires and returns a 401 authentication failure.
- Handles both message-based failures and structured `FailoverError` metadata where `provider: "claude-cli"` is not embedded in the display message.
- Emits the correct recovery command: `openclaw models auth login --provider anthropic --method cli`.
- Review focus: the narrowness of the Claude CLI classification and precedence over generic provider-auth copy.

## Linked context

Fixes #97553.

Related: #97561 overlaps the same root cause; this branch now covers the structured `FailoverError` path called out by ClawSweeper.

Maintainer request: not directly requested by a maintainer.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: expired Claude CLI OAuth failures could surface generic provider-auth guidance instead of the actionable Claude CLI login command.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Node 22 using the agent-runner failure path with typed `FailoverError` inputs that match the subprocess 401 envelope.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node /media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth-refresh-failure.test.ts -- --run --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | grep -E "claude-cli-oauth-proof|✓|×"`
- Evidence after fix: Live terminal output from real node:http loopback server (no fetch mock), Node v22, Linux:

```text
[claude-cli-oauth-proof] server=401 → re-auth hint surfaced: Model login expired on the gateway for claude-cli. Re-auth with `openclaw models auth login --provider anthropic --method cli`, then try again.
[claude-cli-oauth-proof] server=200 → normal response returned
```

- Observed result after fix: the full `runAgentTurnWithFallback` path returns `⚠️ Model login expired on the gateway for claude-cli. Re-auth with \`openclaw models auth login --provider anthropic --method cli\`, then try again.`
- What was not tested: a live expired Claude CLI OAuth session, because this environment does not intentionally hold an expired Claude CLI credential.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof uses the real OpenClaw failure mapping path with typed error envelopes, not a live Telegram/Mantis transcript.
- Before evidence: typed `reason:"auth"` + `status:401` could be caught by generic provider-auth classification before the targeted OAuth-refresh hint.

## Tests and validation

- `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth-refresh-failure.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts -- --run -t "claude-cli"`
- `/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth-refresh-failure.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth-refresh-failure.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`
- New regression coverage includes the structured provider metadata path requested by ClawSweeper.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. A specific Claude CLI 401 now gets a targeted re-auth hint.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes, auth failure classification and recovery guidance changed.

What is the highest-risk area? Misclassifying unrelated provider 401 failures as Claude CLI OAuth expiry.

How is that risk mitigated? Structured classification requires `FailoverError`, `provider: "claude-cli"`, `reason: "auth"`, `status: 401`, plus the Claude authentication failure wording in the message or raw error.

## Current review state

Next action: ClawSweeper/maintainer re-review after the structured metadata fix.

Waiting on: maintainer decision on overlap with #97561; this branch now addresses the gap noted by ClawSweeper.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: handled the raw structured `claude-cli` `FailoverError` path where provider is not embedded in message text.

_AI-assisted; implementation and validation reviewed before pushing._

target.title: fix(sms): guard Twilio JSON.parse against malformed API response bodies
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/sms/src/twilio.ts` has two `JSON.parse()` sites without try/catch on Twilio API response bodies. Malformed JSON → unhandled SyntaxError.

Note: `parseTwilioApiError` (line 85) and `parseTwilioSuccessPayload` (line 104) already have try/catch — only these two sites needed hardening.

## Changes

- `parseTwilioListPayload`: wrap `JSON.parse(text)` in try/catch → return `[]` on failure
- `retrieveTwilioMessagingService`: wrap `JSON.parse(response.text)` in try/catch → throw descriptive Error
- `test/_proof_twilio_json_guard.mts`: **v2** — calls `listTwilioIncomingPhoneNumbers` and `retrieveTwilioMessagingService` with custom `fetchImpl` returning malformed JSON, exercising the actual changed try/catch paths (no vitest or HTTP server needed)

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof — calls changed functions via fetchImpl (L2, v2)

```
$ node --import tsx test/_proof_twilio_json_guard.mts

PASS  listPayload malformed: returns [] (fail-safe) :: result=[]
PASS  messagingService malformed: throws Error :: type=Error
PASS  messagingService malformed: mentions malformed JSON :: msg="Twilio Messaging Service lookup returned malformed JSON."
PASS  listPayload valid: parsed correctly :: count=1 sid=PN123

[proof] 4 PASS, 0 FAIL

target.title: test(android): poll for stale TLS probe cleanup in auth test
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `connect_ignoresStaleTlsProbeAfterDisconnect` used a fixed `Thread.sleep(100)` before asserting async TLS probe cleanup after disconnect.
- Why it matters: Under CI load, 100ms may be insufficient, causing flaky unit test failures.
- What changed: Replaced fixed sleep with a 3s polling loop (10ms interval) that waits until `pendingGatewayTrust` and node-session `desiredBootstrapToken` are both null, with an explicit timeout error.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No production/runtime Android code; only the one test method.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

- [ ] Chore/infra

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Real environment tested: macOS, Android SDK at `~/Library/Android/sdk`, Android Studio JBR 21, Robolectric unit test.

target.title: [codex] Guard doctor repairs for newer configs
target.body:
## Summary

- Block `doctor --fix`, `--yes`, and gateway-token generation from applying repairs when the config was last written by a newer OpenClaw binary.
- Downgrade the doctor prompter to read-only after that guard trips, so later health contributions do not perform repair actions.
- Suppress the generic `Run "openclaw doctor --fix"` hint while version skew is present.

## Why

Running an older checkout against a newer installed Gateway/config can produce stale or misleading repair advice. Doctor should still inspect, but repair guidance and writes should defer to the newer binary unless the existing explicit downgrade override is set.

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts`
- `pnpm vitest run src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor-config-flow.ts src/flows/doctor-health.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/commands/doctor-config-flow.ts src/flows/doctor-health.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Note: `pnpm check:changed` was attempted but widened to all lanes because this worktree already has unrelated `.codex/` and `extensions/canvas/src/host/a2ui/.bundle.hash` changes. It failed in the WhatsApp extension typecheck on existing `baileys`/WhatsApp send-key errors outside this PR scope.

target.title: test: verify Gemini replay drops runtime-context and errored turns
target.body:
## Bug Description

Gemini replay payload construction could include stale/non-user-visible message artifacts in the replay history shape under some prior-turn conditions.

## Root Cause

Replay history needs to exclude:
- `openclaw.runtime-context` custom entries
- assistant turns that failed/errored and should not be replayed back into Gemini contents

## Fix

- add a regression test covering mixed replay history with:
  - runtime-context entries
  - valid assistant turns
  - errored assistant turns
- assert that only valid user/model turns remain in `params.contents`
- make the live-test harness drop legacy `plugins.bundledDiscovery` when copying a real local config into an isolated live-test home, so existing live verification can run end to end in legacy local environments
- add regression coverage for that harness sanitization

## Related Context

This PR adds regression coverage for Gemini replay-history sanitization and the minimal live-test harness compatibility needed to run upstream-facing proof in this environment.

It is adjacent to issue #78838 in the broader Google/Gemini bug cluster, but it does **not** claim to resolve that `rawText` / `assistant_message_end` long-response bug.

## How to Verify

1. Run `pnpm test extensions/google/google-shared.test.ts extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts test/test-env.test.ts`
2. Confirm the focused suites pass
3. Run `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 OPENCLAW_LIVE_TOOL_REPLAY_REPAIR_MODELS=google/gemini-2.5-flash node scripts/test-live.mjs --no-quiet src/agents/tool-replay-repair.live.test.ts`
4. Confirm both live tests pass against Gemini

## Test Plan

- [x] Added regression test for replay-history sanitization
- [x] Added regression test for live-harness config sanitization
- [x] Existing focused tests still pass
- [x] Manual/live verification of the fix completed

## Risk Assessment

Low — the replay change remains test-only, and the additional harness change is isolated to test environment setup for live verification.

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Preserve `fs.glob` no-match parity: a configured glob that matches nothing resolves to an empty set (not the literal pattern), so it no longer emits a phantom missing-file diagnostic; the literal `[pattern]` result is reserved for the no-glob-support fallback only.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`** / **`src/hooks/internal-hook-dispatch.ts`**
- Keep the public `triggerInternalHook(event)` signature (re-exported via `plugin-sdk/hook-runtime`) unchanged and matching `main` — no new public parameter or exported options type.
- Move the bootstrap-only handler scheduling (`yieldBetweenHandlers` with `setImmediate` yield between handlers) and per-handler timing/slow-handler (>500ms) warning into an internal `internal-hook-dispatch` module that is not on the plugin SDK surface.

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable yielding between handlers for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers (via the internal dispatch entrypoint)

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Keep the public `resolveBootstrapFilesForRun` params (re-exported via `plugin-sdk/agent-harness-runtime`) callback-free and matching `main`; route embedded-runner substage timing through an internal `resolveBootstrapFilesForRunWithTiming` variant kept off the SDK surface.
- Support the new async workspace file loading interface.

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning exercised through the internal dispatch entrypoint
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — a no-match glob resolves to an empty set with no missing-file diagnostic (fs.glob parity)

The public plugin SDK surface is unchanged: `node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs` exits 0 with no new package-exported entrypoints. All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: fix(discord): disable ws 8.21.0 receiver part limits for gateway sockets
target.body:
## Summary

ws 8.21.0 introduced `maxBufferedChunks` and `maxFragments` receiver limits (commit `[security] Limit retained message parts`) that can trigger `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and close connections with code 1008 under certain traffic patterns.

This PR explicitly sets both limits to `0` (disabled) for Discord gateway WebSocket connections, restoring the ws 8.20.0 behavior for the trusted Discord gateway peer while preserving the security fix for all other WebSocket usage.

## Changes

- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.ts`**: Base class `createWebSocket()` now passes `maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0` with a `ws.ClientOptions` cast (since `@types/ws` 8.18.1 does not yet expose these fields).
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts`**: Extended `DiscordGatewayWebSocketCtor` type to include the new options; monitor gateway creation path passes the same disabled limits.
- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.test.ts`**: Added test verifying base class WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.test.ts`**: Added test verifying monitor plugin WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.proxy.test.ts`**: Updated two existing exact-match assertions to include the new options.

## Why `0` instead of raising the limits

- ws 8.21.0 treats `0` as disabled (`receiver.js:61`, `receiver.js:98`: `if (this._maxBufferedChunks > 0 && ...)`), matching the pre-8.21.0 behavior exactly.
- Discord gateway is a trusted peer; the security limits are designed to protect against untrusted senders.
- Choosing an arbitrary higher threshold would be fragile without real production traffic data to calibrate against.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: ws 8.21.0's retained-part receiver limits (`maxFragments` default 131072 / `maxBufferedChunks` default 1048576) abort a Discord gateway socket with `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` / close `1008`; this PR sets both to `0` (disabled) on the Discord gateway sockets, restoring ws 8.20.0 behavior for that trusted peer.
- **Real environment tested**: Live Discord gateway connection from a Docker `node:22-bookworm-slim` container (Node `v22.22.3`) with the repo-pinned `ws@8.21.0` installed exactly, using a real bot token (redacted), connecting to `wss://gateway.discord.gg/?v=10&encoding=json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: build a `node:22-bookworm-slim` image with `npm install ws@8.21.0`, then run a client that constructs the gateway socket with this patch's options (`new ws.WebSocket(url, { handshakeTimeout: 30000, maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0 })`), completes the HELLO/IDENTIFY/READY handshake against the real Discord gateway, sends heartbeats, holds a 60s stable window, then closes with code 1000.
- **Evidence after fix**: Live Discord gateway run with this patch's options applied (token, `session_id`, and `resume_gateway_url` redacted):

```
[env] node=v22.22.3 ws=8.21.0
[discord] connecting wss://gateway.discord.gg (maxBufferedChunks:0, maxFragments:0)
[discord] open
[discord] HELLO heartbeat_interval=41250ms
[discord] IDENTIFY sent (token redacted)
[discord] READY received (session_id/resume_gateway_url redacted)
[discord] HEARTBEAT_ACK #1
[discord] stable window 60000ms complete
[discord] close code=1000 reason=<empty>
[result] PASS live: READY + 1 heartbeat ACK; no WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS during stable window
```

Supplemental deterministic mechanism repro against a local ws 8.21.0 gateway streaming one message in 131073 fragments (one over the client default `maxFragments`), exercising the same `ws.WebSocket` receiver path:

```
# default ws.WebSocket (ws 8.21 defaults)
[before] open
[before] error code=WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS message=Too many message fragments
[before] close code=1006 reason=<empty>

# this patch's options applied
[after] open
[after] message received: 131073 bytes - NO receiver-limit close
```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the real Discord gateway, with the disabled limits applied, the connection reaches `READY`, exchanges heartbeats, and stays connected through the 60s window with no `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and no `1008` close; the only close is the intentional client-initiated `1000`. The deterministic repro confirms the same constructor options remove the receiver-limit close that the default ws 8.21 client hits.
- **What was not tested**: the live run confirms a real gateway session stays healthy with the patched options, but does not force Discord's production fragmentation pattern on demand, does not exercise the `zlib-stream` transport, and does not drive the full OpenClaw plugin wiring end-to-end. The deterministic repro proves the ws 8.21 retained-part mechanism and that this patch's options disable the exact receiver checks, but uses a local peer rather than Discord's live framing.

## Tests

- `gateway.test.ts`: 25 passed
- `gateway-plugin.test.ts`: 17 passed  
- `provider.proxy.test.ts`: 21 passed
- TypeScript check (tsgo): clean

Fixes #88330

target.title: fix(proxy): apply enhanced NO_PROXY matching to global undici dispatcher (fixes #95998)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95998.

When `HTTPS_PROXY` is set, the global EnvHttpProxyAgent routes ALL requests through the proxy — including internal plugin requests that should bypass via `NO_PROXY`. The built-in undici NO_PROXY matcher does not support leading-dot subdomain patterns, CIDR notation, or octet wildcards.

OpenClaw already has `matchesNoProxy()` that handles these — but it was only used by provider helpers, not the global dispatcher.

## Changes Made

1. **NO_PROXY-aware proxy dispatcher** — Wraps every EnvHttpProxyAgent with a Proxy that checks `matchesNoProxy()` before dispatching to the direct bypass agent.

2. **Lifecycle synchronization (P1 fix)** — Promise-style `close()`/`destroy()` now await both the bypass agent AND the proxy dispatcher before settling. `destroy(err)` arguments are forwarded correctly.

3. **no_proxy precedence (P1 fix)** — Empty lowercase `no_proxy` now correctly shadows uppercase `NO_PROXY`, matching undici EnvHttpProxyAgent semantics.

## Network Boundary Change — Scope and Rationale

This PR extends OpenClaw existing `matchesNoProxy()` (already used by provider helpers) to the global undici dispatcher. This means NO_PROXY patterns that undici built-in matcher cannot handle (CIDR, octet-wildcard) will now correctly bypass the proxy at the process level.

- **Scope**: Only affects the NO_PROXY matching path. The EnvHttpProxyAgent is still used for all non-bypassed requests. Bypass agent inherits the same timeout/connect policy.
- **Risk**: `matchesNoProxy()` is already proven in production via provider helpers. This PR applies the same logic to the global dispatcher.
- **Upgrade**: Existing deployments with basic domain NO_PROXY patterns are unaffected. Deployments using CIDR or octet-wildcard entries will see those patterns start working (previously broken).
- **Lifecycle safety**: close/destroy synchronizes both dispatchers. Promise-style and callback-style both work correctly.

## Evidence

**Enhanced matcher proof (18/18 tests passed):**
```
$ ./node_modules/.bin/tsx _matcher_proof.mjs

=== Enhanced NO_PROXY Matcher Proof ===
(Core of PR #97713 — the dispatcher wrapper uses matchesNoProxy())

  ✅ Basic: NO_PROXY=example.com, matching: true
  ✅ Basic: NO_PROXY=example.com, non-matching: false
  ✅ Subdomain: .myqcloud.com, COS bucket: true
  ✅ Subdomain: .example.com, deep nested: true
  ✅ Subdomain: .example.com, non-matching: false
  ✅ Wildcard: *.example.com, matching: true
  ✅ Wildcard: *.example.com, non-matching: false
  ✅ CIDR: 10.0.0.0/8, matching: true
  ✅ CIDR: 10.0.0.0/8, non-matching: false
  ✅ CIDR: 100.64.0.0/10, matching (CGNAT): true
  ✅ CIDR: 100.64.0.0/10, non-matching: false
  ✅ CIDR: 169.254.0.0/16, matching (link-local): true
  ✅ CIDR: 169.254.0.0/16, non-matching: false
  ✅ Octet-wildcard: 192.168.*.*, matching: true
  ✅ Octet-wildcard: 192.168.*.*, non-matching: false
  ✅ Precedence: no_proxy shadows NO_PROXY: true
  ✅ Precedence: empty no_proxy blocks uppercase: false
  ✅ Precedence: only NO_PROXY falls back: true

CIDR/Octet-wildcard tests: 8/8 passed
(Undici v8.5.0 built-in NO_PROXY matcher does NOT support CIDR or octet-wildcard patterns)
Total: 18/18 passed (0 failed)
```

**Unit tests (90/90 passed):**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/net/proxy-env.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/net/undici-global-dispatcher.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  35 passed (35)
```

## AI Assistance

- AI-assisted: Yes
- Manually reviewed and verified

## False Positive Risk Mitigation

The enhanced NO_PROXY matcher (`matchesNoProxy()`) is already used by provider helpers in production — this PR extends the same proven logic to the global dispatcher. The false positive risk is inherently limited because:

1. **Pattern-gated**: The enhanced matcher only triggers when the NO_PROXY value actually contains CIDR, octet-wildcard, or leading-dot patterns. For plain domain matching, the behavior is identical to undici built-in.
2. **Already deployed**: Provider helpers (SSRF guard, per-request routing) have used `matchesNoProxy()` in production without incident.
3. **Conservative**: The bypass agent inherits the same timeout and connect policy as the EnvHttpProxyAgent, so bypassed requests behave identically to proxied ones — just without the proxy hop.
4. **Auditable**: The `matchesNoProxy()` logic is contained in a single function (`src/infra/net/proxy-env.ts`) and is covered by 55 unit tests.

Maintainers should explicitly accept the broader proxy-bypass boundary before merge (see Network Boundary section above).


## Proof of Global Dispatcher Routing

The PR changes the global undici dispatcher by wrapping it with a Proxy that checks `matchesNoProxy()` before routing. Each dispatched request follows this flow:

1. `dispatch()` is called on the Proxy wrapper
2. If `hasEnvHttpProxyAgentConfigured() && matchesNoProxy(origin)` → route through **bypass Agent** (direct, no proxy)
3. Otherwise → route through **EnvHttpProxyAgent** (through proxy)

This logic is verified by:

- **35 dispatcher tests** — `undici-global-dispatcher.test.ts` covers proxy wrapper creation, dispatch routing, lifecycle sync, and bypass agent behavior
- **55 proxy-env tests** — `proxy-env.test.ts` covers all `matchesNoProxy()` patterns (CIDR, octet-wildcard, subdomain, precedence)
- **18 matcher scenarios** — verified individually with terminal output above

The 90 unit tests run against the actual undici runtime and confirm the wrapper correctly:
- Forwards matching requests to the bypass agent
- Forwards non-matching requests to the EnvHttpProxyAgent
- Synchronizes close/destroy lifecycle
- Preserves `no_proxy` precedence


## Live Proxy Proof

A local HTTP proxy on 127.0.0.1:18999 was configured as HTTPS_PROXY. The NO_PROXY-aware dispatcher was set as the global undici dispatcher, then fetch() requests were made under different NO_PROXY settings. The proxy logged every CONNECT attempt.

```
1. Without NO_PROXY:
  [proxy] CONNECT example.com:443  ← all through proxy
  [proxy] CONNECT example.com:443
  [proxy] CONNECT example.com:443

2. NO_PROXY=example.com (matching):
  → BYPASS (NO_PROXY match: https://example.com)  ← intercepted

3. NO_PROXY=other.com (non-matching):
  [proxy] CONNECT example.com:443  ← all through proxy
  [proxy] CONNECT example.com:443
  [proxy] CONNECT example.com:443

4. NO_PROXY=100.64.0.0/10 (CIDR — undici lacks this):
  → BYPASS (NO_PROXY match: https://100.100.0.1)  ← CIDR works
```

target.title: fix(qqbot): allow scoped sandbox media sends
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where sandbox-enabled agents could create a media file in their workspace, then fail to send it through QQBot when the reply used the sandbox-visible `/workspace/...` path.

This affects the QQBot media delivery workflow for sandboxed agent replies, including `<qqmedia>` block replies, structured `QQBOT_PAYLOAD` media payloads, and host-read media loading.

## Summary

- Fix classification: Root-cause fix.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High for first-pass review. The current update has fresh QQBot/Core regression tests, type checking, near-real gateway/message-delivery proof for the `/workspace/...` path, a redacted live QQBot C2C media-tag proof, and a passing ClawSweeper commit review.
- Root cause: sandboxed agents see generated files under the virtual root `/workspace`, but the remaining QQBot media paths still treated `/workspace/...` as an absolute host path instead of resolving that virtual root through the scoped agent workspace. That left gateway `<qqmedia>` block replies, structured `QQBOT_PAYLOAD` media paths, and host-read media loading out of sync with the scoped workspace contract.
- Why this is root-cause fix: the fix maps only the sandbox virtual root `/workspace` to the already configured `mediaAccess.workspaceDir` before local media validation or host-read loading. This repairs the source invariant shared by the affected QQBot media entry points: sandbox-visible workspace paths must resolve to the same scoped host workspace that the session authorized.
- Patch quality notes: the additional host-read error handling and `expect.any(...)` assertions are inherited from the existing PR diff and are covered by the focused tests. This update does not add a new fallback root or bypass validation; it preserves the existing scoped local-root checks, adds a traversal regression for `/workspace/../outside-report.docx`, and now preserves already-authorized host `/workspace/...` paths before falling back to sandbox virtual-root mapping. Maintainer feedback on QQBot media skill wording is addressed by making `<qqmedia>{实际路径或URL}</qqmedia>` the generic guidance and documenting `/workspace/...` only as a sandbox-visible path source.

## Linked context

- Related: #92827
- PR rescue target: #92872
- Behavior or issue addressed: sandbox-enabled agents can generate media files in their workspace and send them through QQBot using `/workspace/...` paths in block replies or structured media payloads.
- Why it matters / User impact: users can ask a sandboxed agent to create a document, image, voice file, video, or other supported file and send it through QQBot without the file being rejected only because the agent referenced the sandbox path it can see.
- What did NOT change: this does not add new QQBot upload roots, does not relax scoped media roots, does not change file-size limits, does not add a new public API, and does not claim live QQ group delivery coverage. The live external proof used QQBot C2C because that was the available authorized session.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: the source of truth remains the session-scoped `mediaAccess.workspaceDir`, `mediaAccess.localRoots`, scoped media roots, and OpenClaw media directory. `/workspace` is treated as a virtual alias for the current agent workspace only; all non-workspace absolute paths keep existing behavior.
- Related open PR / same-contract scan: this is an update to the existing linked PR for #92827, not a competing PR. The change stays inside the current QQBot sandbox media contract already under review in #92872.
- Out of scope: no schema/config migration, no new provider/channel contract, no permission broadening for arbitrary host paths, no live external QQ group proof, and no unrelated channel changes.

## Real behavior proof

- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/outbound-media-send.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: Validation `qqbot-gateway-media-focused-postcommit` passed on the submitted commit. A separate redacted live QQBot C2C proof also sent the sandbox-generated `/workspace/live-proof-92872.txt` through the high-level `<qqmedia>` tag path.

  ```text
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-messaging.config.ts

   RUN  v4.1.8 [local path redacted]


   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  41 passed (41)
     Start at  12:27:59
     Duration  13.00s (transform 8.85s, setup 841ms, import 15.77s, tests 292ms, environment 0ms)

  [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 22.75s
  ```

- Observed result after fix:
  - Gateway `<qqmedia>/workspace/sandbox-report.docx</qqmedia>` block replies resolve to the configured agent workspace and reach the QQBot media sender with a scoped local path.
  - Gateway `QQBOT_PAYLOAD` media payloads with `path: "/workspace/payload-workspace-report.pdf"` resolve to the configured agent workspace and reach the QQBot media sender with a scoped local path.
  - Host-read media loading maps `/workspace/report.docx` to the real `mediaAccess.workspaceDir` path before calling `loadOutboundMediaFromUrl`, so sandbox read callbacks receive the host workspace path instead of the virtual path.
  - Already-authorized host paths that literally live under `/workspace/...` are preserved before sandbox virtual-root remapping is tried, so an attachment root such as `/workspace/attachments` is not silently rewritten into another agent workspace.
  - Relative media paths are resolved only against the scoped agent workspace, not against the process current working directory.
  - Escape attempts such as `/workspace/../outside-report.docx` remain blocked and do not call the QQBot media sender.
  - Live QQBot C2C proof: a real sandbox-enabled agent generated `/workspace/live-proof-92872.txt` (49 bytes, content marker `PR 92872 QQBot sandbox workspace media live proof`). Sending `<qqmedia>/workspace/live-proof-92872.txt</qqmedia>` through the QQBot high-level tag path returned QQ API `200 OK` for upload and send, with a QQBot message id returned. App id, secret, provider key, token, full sender id, and full message ids are redacted.
- What was not tested: no live external QQ group delivery was exercised in this environment because the available authorized external QQBot session was C2C. The proof combines local near-real gateway/message-delivery behavior, host-read behavior, and redacted live QQBot C2C media-tag delivery on the submitted diff.

## Tests and validation

- Target test file: `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts`
  - Scenario locked in: a gateway block reply with `<qqmedia>/workspace/sandbox-report.docx</qqmedia>` resolves through the configured agent workspace and reaches the QQBot sender.
  - Scenario locked in: a gateway block reply with `<qqmedia>/workspace/../outside-report.docx</qqmedia>` remains blocked by scoped local-root validation.
  - Scenario locked in: a structured `QQBOT_PAYLOAD` media payload with `/workspace/...` resolves through the configured agent workspace and reaches the QQBot sender.
- Target test file: `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/outbound-media-send.test.ts`
  - Scenario locked in: host-read media loading receives the mapped host workspace path for `/workspace/report.docx` before calling `loadOutboundMediaFromUrl`.
  - Scenario locked in: an already-authorized host path under `/workspace/attachments` is preserved before virtual workspace mapping is attempted.
  - Scenario locked in: relative outbound media paths are resolved only against `mediaAccess.workspaceDir`.
  - Scenario locked in: relative voice host-read paths still resolve inside `mediaAccess.workspaceDir` before staging the QQBot voice upload.
- Target test file: `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/reply-dispatcher.test.ts`
  - Scenario locked in: structured `QQBOT_PAYLOAD` local file paths preserve already-authorized host `/workspace/...` roots before virtual workspace mapping.
  - Scenario locked in: relative structured payload paths are resolved only against `mediaAccess.workspaceDir`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the tests exercise the same QQBot gateway, structured-payload, and host-read boundaries that previously rejected or rewrote sandbox media paths, while keeping traversal behavior covered at the security boundary.

Validation after the previous submitted commit and this audit update:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/outbound-media-send.test.ts`

  ```text
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
       Tests  41 passed (41)
  ```

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qqbot/src/channel.message-adapter.test.ts`

  ```text
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  7 passed (7)
  ```

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/message-action-params.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.media.test.ts`

  ```text
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
       Tests  65 passed (65)
  ```

- `corepack pnpm check:test-types`

  ```text
  $ pnpm tsgo:test
  $ pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
  $ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
  $ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
  ```

Additional validation after the path-order audit update:

- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/outbound-media-send.test.ts extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/reply-dispatcher.test.ts extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts --reporter=dot`

  ```text
  Test Files  3 passed (3)
       Tests  46 passed (46)
  ```

- `corepack pnpm check:test-types`

  ```text
  passed
  ```

Validation for the maintainer-requested QQBot media skill wording update:

- `python -c 'from pathlib import Path; text=Path("extensions/qqbot/skills/qqbot-media/SKILL.md").read_text(); assert "<qqmedia>{实际路径或URL}</qqmedia>" in text; assert "<qqmedia>/workspace/文件名或URL</qqmedia>" not in text; assert "只有当沙箱 workspace-write 创建结果实际显示 `/workspace/...`" in text; assert "附件路径" in text; assert "URL 可以直接发送" in text; assert "QQBot 媒体目录" in text; print("skill wording ok")'`

  ```text
  skill wording ok
  ```

## Risk checklist

- Risk labels considered: `merge-risk: message-delivery`, `merge-risk: security-boundary`, plus compatibility/availability/session-state signals from the QQBot outbound media path.
- Risk explanation: QQBot media delivery sits on a security-sensitive boundary because it turns an agent reply path into host file reads and outbound uploads. Mapping `/workspace` incorrectly could either keep breaking sandbox sends or accidentally allow paths outside the session workspace.
- Why acceptable: already-authorized absolute paths are checked first, sandbox virtual-root mapping is limited to the literal `/workspace` root as a fallback, and the existing scoped local-root checks still decide whether the resulting host path is allowed. The regression suite covers the allowed workspace case, the already-authorized host `/workspace/...` case, relative path resolution, and the rejected traversal case; the fix does not add a new trusted root.
- Compatibility/default behavior: relative paths continue to resolve inside the scoped workspace; ordinary absolute host paths keep existing behavior, including literal `/workspace/...` host paths that are already authorized by scoped roots; remote URLs and data sources continue through the existing URL/data handling.
- Large diff note: the overall PR is already larger because it previously threaded scoped media access across QQBot outbound paths. This update is the narrow follow-up requested by review: `/workspace` mapping, gateway/host-read regressions, and skill wording alignment.

## Current review state

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001 `status: ⏳ waiting on author`: addressed by this update path, but the label should be removed only by the normal remote review/automation after submit. I did not manually edit labels.
- RF-002 Map `/workspace/...` media references and add gateway `<qqmedia>` regression: fixed. Added gateway block reply coverage for `/workspace/sandbox-report.docx` resolving to the configured agent workspace.
- RF-003 Security-boundary advisory for localRoots/readFile after `/workspace` mapping: disclosed and covered. `/workspace/...` maps to `mediaAccess.workspaceDir`, while `/workspace/../outside-report.docx` remains blocked by scoped local-root validation.
- RF-004 Live proof gap for sandbox-generated `/workspace/...` QQBot media or QQ group send: addressed for live QQBot C2C media delivery and local near-real gateway/message-delivery coverage. A real sandbox-enabled agent generated `/workspace/live-proof-92872.txt`; the same `/workspace/...` path was sent through the high-level `<qqmedia>` QQBot tag path and returned QQ API `200 OK` for upload and send. Live QQ group proof is explicitly not claimed because the available authorized external QQBot session was C2C.
- RF-005 Narrow repair for remaining absolute sandbox workspace path and stale QQBot media skill wording: fixed. The QQBot skill now presents `<qqmedia>{实际路径或URL}</qqmedia>` as the generic format; `/workspace/...` is documented only as a possible path an agent may actually see in sandbox workspace-write runs. Attachment-context paths and URLs are kept direct, and local paths remain limited to authorized scoped roots.
- RF-006 Map `/workspace` media tags through the scoped workspace in `outbound-media-send.ts`: fixed. Host-read paths are mapped before `loadOutboundMediaFromUrl`, including the daily-limit metadata path.
- RF-007 Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/outbound-media-send.test.ts`: passed after the submitted commit.
- RF-008 Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qqbot/src/channel.message-adapter.test.ts`: passed after the submitted commit.
- RF-009 Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/message-action-params.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.media.test.ts`: passed after the submitted commit.
- RF-010 Run `corepack pnpm check:test-types`: passed after the submitted commit.
- RF-011 Maintainer feedback from `sliverp` on QQBot media skill wording: fixed. The skill no longer teaches `/workspace/...` as the universal media format; it uses `<qqmedia>{实际路径或URL}</qqmedia>` and explains how to choose the path source for sandbox-created files, attachment-context paths, direct URLs, and authorized local media roots.
- RF-012 Additional path-order audit finding: fixed. Literal host paths already authorized under `/workspace/...` are now validated before sandbox virtual-root remapping is attempted, while relative paths still resolve only inside the scoped agent workspace.

Commit review state:

- ClawSweeper commit review: passed on the final single-commit update; no blocking findings.

<details>
<summary>Summary guidance</summary>

This PR description is the contributor's durable explanation of the change. Write it for human maintainers first; ClawSweeper and Barnacle use the same text to understand intent, proof, risk, and current review state.

Describe the intent and outcome in 2-5 bullets. Avoid restating the diff; reviewers and bots can read the changed files.

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

</details>

## Linked context

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

<details>
<summary>Linked context guidance</summary>

Link the issue, PR, discussion, maintainer request, or owner request that explains why this PR should exist. Maintainer context helps reviewers and automation distinguish intended work from drive-by churn.

</details>

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
- Real environment tested:
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
- Observed result after fix:
- What was not tested:
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

<details>
<summary>Real behavior proof guidance</summary>

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only.

Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes. Terminal screenshots, copied live output, redacted runtime logs, recordings, and linked artifacts count.

If your environment cannot produce the ideal proof, explain that under `Proof limitations or environment constraints` so reviewers and ClawSweeper can direct the next step properly.

Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

</details>

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?


What regression coverage was added or updated?


What failed before this fix, if known?


If no test was added, why not?

<details>
<summary>Testing guidance</summary>

List focused commands, not every incidental check. CI is useful support, but external PRs still need real behavior proof above when behavior changes.

</details>

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)


Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)


Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)


What is the highest-risk area?


How is that risk mitigated?

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

Use this for author judgment that is not obvious from the diff. ClawSweeper can see touched files, but it cannot know which behavior you think is risky, why the risk is acceptable, or what mitigation reviewers should verify.

</details>

<details>

Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

target.title: fix(sms): guard Twilio JSON.parse against malformed API response bodies
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/sms/src/twilio.ts` has two `JSON.parse()` sites without try/catch on Twilio API response bodies. Malformed JSON → unhandled SyntaxError.

Note: `parseTwilioApiError` (line 85) and `parseTwilioSuccessPayload` (line 104) already have try/catch — only these two sites needed hardening.

## Changes

- `parseTwilioListPayload`: wrap `JSON.parse(text)` in try/catch → return `[]` on failure
- `retrieveTwilioMessagingService`: wrap `JSON.parse(response.text)` in try/catch → throw descriptive Error
- `test/_proof_twilio_json_guard.mts`: **v2** — calls `listTwilioIncomingPhoneNumbers` and `retrieveTwilioMessagingService` with custom `fetchImpl` returning malformed JSON, exercising the actual changed try/catch paths (no vitest or HTTP server needed)

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof — calls changed functions via fetchImpl (L2, v2)

```
$ node --import tsx test/_proof_twilio_json_guard.mts

PASS  listPayload malformed: returns [] (fail-safe) :: result=[]
PASS  messagingService malformed: throws Error :: type=Error
PASS  messagingService malformed: mentions malformed JSON :: msg="Twilio Messaging Service lookup returned malformed JSON."
PASS  listPayload valid: parsed correctly :: count=1 sid=PN123

[proof] 4 PASS, 0 FAIL
```

### Unit tests — via fetchImpl injection

```
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/sms/src/twilio.test.ts

 ✓ rejects malformed JSON from Twilio Messaging Service lookup
 ✓ returns empty list on malformed JSON from Twilio incoming phone number list
 ... (all existing tests pass)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
```

### v2 fix

v1 had only unit test output → `silver shellfish`. v2 adds proof script using `fetchImpl` injection (discord v3 pattern) — calls the actual changed functions without mocking.

target.title: fix(agents): allow empty-error-retry when replayMetadata is absent (#97877)
target.body: Fixes #97877. ## What Problem This Solves `replayMetadata.hadPotentialSideEffects` is accumulated across attempts via `observeReplayMetadata`. When a session executes tools on Turn 1 and then hits a transient provider 5xx on Turn 2 (before any tool execution in the current attempt), the cumulative flag is already `true` from Turn 1 — incorrectly blocking the empty-error-retry path even though the failed model call itself never ran tools. ## Why This Change Was Made Four changes to separate current-attempt side effects from cumulative session history: 1. **EmbeddedRunAttemptResult** (types.ts): Added optional `currentAttemptReplayMetadata` — records side effects from the current attempt only. 2. **normalizeEmbeddedRunAttemptResult** (run.ts): Computes `currentAttemptReplayMetadata` from per-attempt raw fields via `buildAttemptReplayMetadata` — the same function `attempt.ts` uses to compute `observedReplayMetadata` before accumulation. The cumulative `replayMetadata` field stays required via `resolveAttemptReplayMetadata(raw)` — no contract weakening. 3. **shouldRetrySilentErrorAssistantTurn** (incomplete-turn.ts): Prefers `currentAttemptReplayMetadata` to decide whether this specific model call had side effects, falling back to cumulative `replayMetadata` for legacy harnesses. 4. **Runner proof + gateway proof**: Test harness captures `[empty-error-retry]` via `mockedLog.warn`. Live gateway agent with local proxy demonstrates recovery from provider error after prior-turn tool execution. ## User Impact Agents recover from transient 5xx provider errors on any turn — even after prior turns executed tools. The documented `[empty-error-retry]` retry of up to 3 attempts fires when the failed model call itself produced no content, no tool calls, and no delivery side effects. ## Evidence **Behavior addressed**: Cumulative `replayMetadata.hadPotentialSideEffects` from prior turns blocks retry of a transient 5xx on a later turn where the model failed before tool execution. **Real environment tested**: Node 22.19+, local OpenClaw checkout. Two proof levels: (1) runner harness with real `runEmbeddedAgent` → `log.warn` capture, (2) live gateway agent with local proxy + real ZTE provider. **Proof 1 — Runner-level: `[empty-error-retry]` log capture** ``` node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.empty-error-retry.test.ts --run ``` ``` Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 23 passed (23) ``` Key assertions from the real `runEmbeddedAgent` path: ``` ✓ retry on 5xx → mockedLog.warn called with "[empty-error-retry]..." ✓ prior-turn tools + clean 5xx → mockedLog.warn called with "[empty-error-retry]..." (#97877 fix) ✓ current-turn tools → mockedLog.warn NOT called (no duplicate side effects) ``` Only `runEmbeddedAttempt` (HTTP call) is mocked; `normalizeEmbeddedRunAttemptResult`, `shouldRetrySilentErrorAssistantTurn`, retry loop, and `log.warn` are all production code. **Proof 2 — Gateway-level: Live agent recovery with real provider** Proxy intercepts chat #3 (Turn 2 first attempt) with plaintext error. Real ZTE Qwen3-235B-A22B API handles all other requests. ``` === Turn 1: run tool → cumulative side effects === [proxy] chat #1 → real API (model decides to run echo hello) [proxy] chat #2 → real API (tool result continuation) hello ← tool output [agent] run ... ended with stopReason=stop === Turn 2: provider error → retry → recovery === [proxy] chat #3 → plaintext body → unclassified → [empty-error-retry] response status=200 contentType=text/plain (not a transport error → runner retry) [proxy] chat #4 → real API (retry after [empty-error-retry]) response status=200 contentType=text/event-stream OK ← recovered reply [agent] run ... ended with stopReason=stop ``` Key observations: - Turn 1 used 2 chats (model call + tool continuation) → side effects accumulated - Turn 2 used 2 chats (plaintext error → retry → real API) instead of 1 - The plaintext body returned HTTP 200 — transport layer does NOT retry HTTP 200 - The runner-level `shouldRetrySilentErrorAssistantTurn` + `currentAttemptReplayMetadata` clean-check allowed retry despite cumulative side effects from Turn 1 - No duplicate tools or side effects — only 4 total chats for 2 turns **Behavior proof (5 scenario matrix)**: ``` [1] currentAttempt clean + cumulative dirty (PRIOR TOOLS) → retry YES ✅ [2] currentAttempt dirty + cumulative dirty (TOOLS RAN) → retry NO ✅ [3] LEGACY (no currentAttempt), cumulative dirty → retry NO ✅ [4] No metadata at all (5xx before tool subsystem) → retry YES ✅ [5] Both clean (clean session) → retry YES ✅ ``` ``` node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.empty-error-retry.test.ts --run Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 23 passed (23) node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts --run Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 114 passed (114) node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts ... (all 5 changed files) (passed, exit 0) ``` **Observed result after fix**: The `runEmbeddedAgent` runner produces `[empty-error-retry]` logs when retrying. In the live gateway, a plaintext provider error on Turn 2 (after Turn 1's tool execution) triggers runner-level retry — the agent calls the model again and successfully recovers with `stopReason=stop`. Legacy harnesses without `currentAttemptReplayMetadata` fall back to cumulative `replayMetadata` (backward compatible, no retry when cumulative is dirty). **What was not tested**: Live E2E with a real 5xx HTTP status from the provider. The gateway proof used HTTP 200 + unparseable body (text/plain) to trigger `stopReason="error"` without transport-level retry — this exercises the exact same `unclassified` failover path that a real provider crash would take. ## Regression Test Plan - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.empty-error-retry.test.ts --run` — 23 passed - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts --run` — 114 passed - `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on all changed files — exit 0 ## Root Cause In `attempt.ts`, `replayMetadataFromState(observeReplayMetadata(getReplayState(), observedReplayMetadata))` merges per-attempt `observedReplayMetadata` with accumulated session-level state before returning. The resulting `rawAttempt.replayMetadata` is always cumulative — `hadPotentialSideEffects` carries the OR of all prior turns. Previously `normalizeEmbeddedRunAttemptResult` saved `raw.replayMetadata` as `currentAttemptReplayMetadata`, making both fields carry the same cumulative value — the per-attempt check was a no-op. The fix re-derives per-attempt metadata from the raw attempt's observable fields (toolMetas, messaging delivery evidence, session spawns, cron adds) using `buildAttemptReplayMetadata` — the same function `attempt.ts` uses to compute `observedReplayMetadata` before accumulation. ## AI Assistance - **AI-assisted**: Yes - **Model used**: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) - **Co-Authored-By**: Already in commit message

target.title: fix(mattermost): backfill thread history from server when in-memory window is empty
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where Mattermost users replying in an existing thread could lose earlier thread context after the OpenClaw monitor restarted or the agent session was reset.

Fixes #93204

## Why This Change Was Made

The Mattermost monitor now backfills a cold local thread-history window from the Mattermost thread endpoint before building inbound context. The backfill marker is keyed by the stored thread `sessionId`, not the stable route session key, so a real `/new` or session reset in a thread gets its own recovery fetch while same-session follow-ups avoid repeated server fetches.

## User Impact

Mattermost thread replies keep useful prior context after restart or `/new`, without adding repeated thread-history requests to normal same-session follow-ups.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.test.ts`: 72 passed; covers stable `route.sessionKey`, stored thread `sessionId` rotation, same-session no-refetch, and reset-session refetch.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`: passed.
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts`: passed.
- `git diff --check`: passed.

Live proof: local Docker Mattermost Team Edition 10.11.21 at `http://localhost:8065`, with tokens and Mattermost ids redacted. The proof used real Mattermost REST auth, posts, and `/api/v4/posts/<root>/thread` through `monitorMattermostProvider`; a fake websocket only injected `posted` events into the monitor. The route/thread session key stayed stable while the stored thread `sessionId` rotated. Cold start included prior root/thread context, same-session server-only context stayed absent, and stored-thread-session reset included new server-only prior context:

```json
{
  "mattermost": "10.11.21 local Docker, token/id redacted",
  "routeSessionKey": "stable, redacted",
  "threadSessionKey": "stable, redacted",
  "threadSessionKeyStable": true,
  "storedThreadSessionIds": [
    "stored-session-before-reset",
    "stored-session-after-reset"
  ],
  "dispatchCount": 3,
  "firstColdStartContainsPriorThreadContext": true,
  "firstColdStartContainsRootContext": true,
  "sameSessionSkippedServerOnlyContext": true,
  "storedThreadSessionIdResetContainsNewPriorContext": true
}
```

target.title: feat: add JSON-RPC plugin bindings
target.body:
Closes #98004

## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw plugin authors who want to implement runtime logic in Rust, Python, Go, or another non-TypeScript language currently need to handroll process bridges behind each plugin surface. That duplicates stdio framing, lifecycle cleanup, timeout handling, environment inheritance policy, HTTP body handling, and Gateway response mapping across plugins.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds a public `openclaw/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin` entrypoint with `defineJsonRpcPluginEntry`. The TypeScript entry remains the synchronous source of truth for OpenClaw plugin descriptors, while invoked tool, hook, HTTP route, and Gateway method calls dispatch to a lazily started child process over newline-framed JSON-RPC 2.0 on stdio. The implementation keeps existing OpenClaw auth/scope gates at the registered host surface and avoids introducing a socket listener.

## User Impact

Plugin authors can now build language-agnostic plugin runtimes while still using OpenClaw's existing plugin architecture and manifest model. Rust or other native runtimes only need a small JavaScript descriptor file plus a JSON-RPC stdio server.

AI-assisted: yes. I understand the code change and validated the touched SDK boundary.

## Evidence

- `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check`
- `pnpm check:docs`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.ts src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings

Earlier full SDK proof on this branch also passed:

- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:check-exports`
- `pnpm docs:list`

Implementation size: 12 files changed, +1362/-7 total. Public runtime code is one new SDK helper (`src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.ts`, 650 LOC) plus focused tests (`src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.test.ts`, 585 LOC); the rest is export metadata, API/surface/docs generated metadata, and docs.

target.title: fix(gateway): yield during embedded agent prep
target.body:
## Summary

  - Problem: embedded agent preparation can run heavy synchronous prep blocks in the Gateway process before model
  execution.
  - Why it matters: under load, cheap Gateway/WebSocket requests can become coupled to long agent prep work,
  contributing to poor responsiveness like the symptoms reported in #78861.
  - What changed: added a private cooperative `setImmediate` prep-yield helper and inserted safe yield checkpoints
  after core tool construction, bootstrap context, and bundled tool preparation.
  - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no worker threads, priority queue, caching, lazy loading, protocol/config
  changes, plugin/channel behavior, or public API changes.

  ## Change Type (select all)

  - [x] Bug fix
  - [ ] Feature
  - [ ] Refactor required for the fix
  - [ ] Docs
  - [ ] Security hardening
  - [ ] Chore/infra

  ## Scope (select all touched areas)

  - [x] Gateway / orchestration
  - [x] Skills / tool execution
  - [ ] Auth / tokens
  - [ ] Memory / storage
  - [ ] Integrations
  - [ ] API / contracts
  - [ ] UI / DX
  - [ ] CI/CD / infra

  ## Linked Issue/PR

  - Closes #
  - Related #78861
  - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

  ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

  - Behavior or issue addressed: Gateway responsiveness during embedded agent preparation.
  - Real environment tested: Local macOS development checkout; no live reporter-like Windows/provider setup was
  available.
  - Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test ...`, `pnpm build`, targeted formatter, and changed-lane
  gate attempt.
  - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked
  artifact, or copied live output): terminal output from targeted tests and build showing passing results.
  - Observed result after fix: cheap Gateway request handling is covered by a regression test proving it is not
  coupled to agent completion after accepted dispatch.
  - What was not tested: reporter’s Windows 10 / Node 25.9.0 / private provider config and live WebSocket latency
  under real load.
  - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): #78861 issue logs.

  ## Root Cause (if applicable)

  - Root cause: embedded agent prep performs several potentially expensive preparation phases inside the Gateway event
  loop before model execution, with limited cooperative scheduling between phases.
  - Missing detection / guardrail: no focused regression test proving cheap Gateway requests remain serviceable while
  an accepted agent run is still pending.
  - Contributing context (if known): #78861 reports long prep-stage timings in `core-plugin-tools`, `bootstrap-
  context`, `bundle-tools`, and `system-prompt`.

  ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

  - Coverage level that should have caught this:
    - [x] Unit test
    - [x] Seam / integration test
    - [ ] End-to-end test
    - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
  - Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/
  attempt.test.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-prep-yield.test.ts`
  - Scenario the test should lock in: accepted agent dispatch yields before heavy work, cheap Gateway requests can be
  served while the agent result is pending, and prep checkpoint continuations await the injected yield before model
  execution proceeds.
  - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it verifies the exact scheduling seam without starting a real
  Gateway, provider, or channel runtime.
  - Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing accepted-ack yield coverage in `src/gateway/server-
  methods/agent.test.ts`.
  - If no new test is added, why not: N/A; new tests were added.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes

  Gateway/Control UI responsiveness should improve during embedded agent prep because the runner now cooperatively
  yields between major prep phases. No config or API changes.

  ## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```text
  Before:
  [agent accepted] -> [core tools + bootstrap + bundle tools prep in one long run] -> [model execution]  -> [cheap gateway request waits for event loop opportunity]

  After:
  [agent accepted] -> [core tools] -> [yield] -> [bootstrap] -> [yield] -> [bundle tools] -> [yield] -> [model execution] -> [cheap gateway request can run between prep phases]
  ```

  ## Security Impact (required)

  - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) No
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No
  - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No
  - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No
  - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment

  - OS: macOS local development environment
  - Runtime/container: local Node/pnpm workspace
  - Model/provider: mocked/local tests only
  - Integration/channel (if any): none
  - Relevant config (redacted): default test config/mocks

  ### Steps

  1. Run targeted Gateway and agent runner tests.
  2. Run targeted formatter and git diff --check.
  3. Run pnpm build.
  4. Run pnpm changed:lanes --json and pnpm check:changed.

  ### Expected

  - Targeted tests pass.
  - Build passes.
  - Changed gate either passes or reports only unrelated actionable failures.

  ### Actual

  - Targeted tests passed.
  - pnpm build passed.
  - pnpm check:changed failed in unrelated existing core test typecheck fixtures outside this diff.

  ## Evidence

  Attach at least one:

  - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
  - [ ] Trace/log snippets
  - [ ] Screenshot/recording
  - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

  Passing after:

  - pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts
  - pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-prep-yield.test.ts
  - pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts
  - pnpm test src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/gateway/server/event-loop-health.test.ts src/gateway/server-
    methods/agent.test.ts
  - pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-prep-
    yield.test.ts
  - pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/
    attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-prep-
    yield.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-prep-yield.test.ts
  - git diff --check
  - pnpm build

  Known gate caveat:

  - pnpm check:changed failed after pnpm install in unrelated existing test fixture type errors:
      - src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts
      - src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/openai-stream-wrappers.test.ts

  ## Human Verification (required)

  What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

  - Verified scenarios: accepted agent dispatch still responds first; cheap Gateway request can be handled while agent
    result is pending; prep yield helper budgets and resets correctly; prep checkpoint awaits the injected yield
    before model execution continuation.
  - Edge cases checked: helper does not yield before budget; helper yields after budget; helper reset restarts
    accounting.
  - What you did not verify: live reporter environment, Windows-specific behavior, real provider latency, real
    WebSocket latency under high-load production config.

  ## Review Conversations

  - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

  If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review
  conversation cleanup for maintainers.

  ## Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes
  - Config/env changes? (Yes/No) No
  - Migration needed? (Yes/No) No
  - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

  ## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: cooperative yields can slightly increase best-case prep latency by adding event-loop turns.
      - Mitigation: yields are limited to three major prep boundaries before locks, transcript mutation, provider
        stream handling, and model/tool execution.
  - Risk: this does not fully solve the broader architecture requested in #78861.
      - Mitigation: PR is intentionally scoped as a small responsiveness mitigation; worker isolation, priority
        scheduling, caching, and lazy loading remain separate maintainer-level work.

### Built with Codex
