target.title: fix(cli): add configure and onboard to interactive TTY command skip list
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw configure` and `openclaw onboard` hang and leave high-CPU child processes on some Linux kernel/Node.js combinations. Reported in #83560.

## Root Cause

The ExperimentalWarning suppression respawn in `buildCliRespawnPlan` wraps the command in a parent/child process pair sharing the same TTY fd. The parent installs signal handlers via `attachChildProcessBridge`, keeping the event loop alive. The child's clack `select()` calls `stdin.setRawMode(true)` on the shared fd. On Debian kernel 6.12.63 + Node 22.22.1 this causes the child to busy-wait at high CPU or hang indefinitely.

## Fix

Add `configure` and `onboard` to the existing `INTERACTIVE_TTY_COMMANDS` set in `respawn-policy.ts`. This makes `shouldSkipRespawnForArgv` return `true` for both, matching the existing treatment of `tui`, `terminal`, and `chat`. Because the function is driven by `resolveCliArgvInvocation(argv).primary` (via `getPrimaryCommand`), root options before the subcommand are already handled correctly — e.g. `openclaw --profile server configure` works without any additional argv-position logic.

`shouldSkipStartupEnvironmentRespawnForArgv` is intentionally **not** changed: the CA-certs startup-environment respawn is safe for interactive commands because it only injects an env var without altering stdin/stdout or installing a signal bridge. Tests confirm `configure`/`onboard` still allow that respawn path.

## Changes

- `src/cli/respawn-policy.ts` (+1 line): add `"configure"` and `"onboard"` to `INTERACTIVE_TTY_COMMANDS`
- `src/cli/cli-utils.test.ts` (+23 lines): extend the existing `shouldSkipRespawnForArgv` test table with `configure`, `onboard`, and root-option variants; add a new describe block asserting `shouldSkipStartupEnvironmentRespawnForArgv` returns `false` for these commands

## Reviewer checklist

- Branch now based on upstream `main` @ `ff871e162a`
- Root-option invocations covered: `openclaw --profile server configure`, `openclaw --profile default onboard`
- CA-certs respawn behavior explicitly tested and unchanged
- No new argv-position parser introduced; existing `resolveCliArgvInvocation` does the work

## Acceptance criteria (for live repro)

```bash
timeout 12 script -qefc 'TERM=xterm-256color OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/oc-repro openclaw configure' /tmp/oc-repro.log
# Must exit cleanly; no high-CPU child processes remaining after exit
```

Fixes #83560

target.title: feat(comfy): dynamic aspect ratio → width/height injection
target.body:
## What Make the Comfy image generation tool respect `aspectRatio` and `size` parameters instead of ignoring them due to hard-coded dimensions in the workflow JSON. **This change also makes the `capabilities` config on all image providers dynamic**, not just Comfy. The core provider types, plugin-sdk exports, and image-generate tool were updated to support runtime capability resolution. ## Changes - **workflow-runtime.ts** - Added `ComfyDimensionConfig` type for target node configuration (`widthNodeId`, `heightNodeId`, input names) - Added `calculateDimensions()` — derives width/height from aspect ratio or size using a configurable base resolution (default 1024px), rounded to 64px increments per ComfyUI requirements - Added `setWorkflowDimensionInput()` — injects calculated dimensions into the workflow JSON at configured node(s) before submission - `runComfyWorkflow()` now accepts `aspectRatio` and `size` params - **image-generation-provider.ts** (Comfy) - Enabled `supportsAspectRatio: true` and `supportsSize: true` in generate capabilities - Passes both params through to `runComfyWorkflow()` - Capabilities are now resolved dynamically via `isComfyDimensionsConfigured()` - **openclaw.plugin.json** (Comfy) - Added `dimensions` config object under image capability schema (includes optional `baseSize`) - Made `widthNodeId`/`heightNodeId` required fields in the schema - **src/image-generation/types.ts** — all image providers affected - Added `ImageGenerationCapabilitiesResolver` type - `capabilities` field on `ImageGenerationProvider` is now `ImageGenerationProviderCapabilities | ImageGenerationCapabilitiesResolver` (was just `ImageGenerationProviderCapabilities`) - Added `resolveProviderCapabilities()` helper function - **src/plugin-sdk/image-generation.ts** — all providers affected - New exports: `resolveProviderCapabilities`, `ImageGenerationCapabilitiesResolver`, `ImageGenerationProviderCapabilities` - **src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts** — all providers affected - Updated to use `resolveProviderCapabilities()` for capability checks - `validateImageGenerationCapabilities` now accepts optional `ctx` param for dynamic resolution - Both generate and edit path capability lookups go through the resolver ## Configuring Add a `dimensions` block to your Comfy image workflow config pointing at the node that controls output size: ```json "image": { "workflowPath": "...", "promptNodeId": "42", "dimensions": { "widthNodeId": "10", "heightNodeId": "10", "baseSize": 768 } } ``` - `widthNodeId` / `heightNodeId`: node IDs in your workflow JSON (can be the same node for joint width/height inputs) - `widthInputName` / `heightInputName`: input field names within those nodes (defaults: "width", "height") - `baseSize`: base resolution for aspect ratio calculation, defaults to 1024. Use 512 for SD 1.5, 768 for SDXL, etc. ## Backwards compatibility Falls back cleanly — no config or request changes needed: 1. No aspectRatio/size on request → dimension block skipped entirely 2. aspectRatio/size provided but no `dimensions` config → no injection, workflow unchanged 3. Invalid aspect ratio → `calculateDimensions()` returns null, no injection 4. Static `capabilities` object (no resolver function) → used as-is 5. `capabilities` resolved without context → same fallback behavior ## Tests - Explicit size injection into configured nodes - Calculated dimensions from aspect ratio (16:9 at base 1024 = 1024x576) - Size precedence over aspectRatio when both provided - Fallback when no dimensions config is present - Invalid aspect ratio gracefully ignored - Custom baseSize respected ## Real behavior proof

target.title: fix(memory-core): treat dreaming fence marker lines as inside-fence in promotion guard (#80613)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** `lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence` in `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts` tracked `insideFence` state from the fence marker lines but never flagged ranges that included the marker lines themselves. A relocated promotion range that ended on a `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:start -->` line, began on a `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:end -->` line, or covered only a marker line passed the guard, and the snippet built from those raw lines carried the marker text into `MEMORY.md`.
- **Why it matters.** The guard's contract is to refuse rehydrated ranges that overlap managed dreaming fences so dream artifacts cannot be promoted. Treating the marker lines themselves as outside-the-fence leaves a narrow but real leak: any window the relocator picks that clips a marker produces a snippet that includes the literal `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:start/end -->` text, and (when the window straddles content on either side of the marker) the adjacent line. This is the residual edge case noted by ClawSweeper on #80702 ("the managed-block leak half is related but should be handled by a focused memory-core promotion fix"), narrowed to the guard itself rather than a parallel sanitizer.
- **What changed.** `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts`: marker lines are now checked against the range bounds; if a marker is inside the relocated range, the range is rejected. The pre-existing inside-fence tracking remains so interior lines between a start/end pair are still flagged. The contract elsewhere — the contamination predicate, the relocator, the apply path, the `MEMORY.md` writer — is untouched.
- **What did not change (scope boundary).** No change to `relocateCandidateRange`, `isContaminatedDreamingSnippet`, `applyShortTermPromotions`, `buildDailySnippetChunks`, `stripManagedDailyDreamingLines`, the CJK dedupe path (the second sub-bug of #80613 is being addressed separately by #80620), or any `MEMORY.md` rendering. No new sanitizer is introduced — this is a narrowing of the existing guard.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Refs #80613 (does not fully close; the dramatic full-Candidate leak from the issue body is already prevented by the merged fence-overlap guard in #68774. This PR narrows a residual marker-line edge case in the same guard.)
- Related: #80620 (CJK dedupe half, separate PR), #80702 (closed sanitizer approach, intentionally not duplicated here).
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence` now flags relocated promotion ranges that include a dreaming fence marker line, preventing the marker text (and any adjacent fenced content captured by the same raw-line window) from being promoted into `MEMORY.md`.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS (Apple Silicon, Node v22.14.0, pnpm 11.1.0). The fix is mechanical and exercised by Vitest unit tests against the helper directly.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts -t "lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence"` — targeted run of the affected describe block.
  - `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — full file regression.
  - `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts` — adjacent file regression.
  - `pnpm format:check extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — format on changed files.
  - `pnpm lint extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — lint on changed files.
  - `pnpm tsgo:extensions` and `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — typecheck.
- Evidence after fix:
  - Before the source change, the four added tests reproduce the gap (output captured locally):
    ```
    Tests  4 failed | 4 passed | 50 skipped (58)
    × returns true when the range ends on a Light Sleep start marker
    × returns true when the range begins on a Light Sleep end marker
    × returns true when the range covers only a marker line
    × returns true for REM marker single-line ranges even with no body between markers
    ```
    Each failure form: `expected false to be true`, confirming that the pre-patch guard returns `false` for a range that includes only a marker line.
  - After the source change:
    ```
    Tests  8 passed | 50 skipped (58)            # lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence -t filter
    Tests  58 passed (58)                         # full short-term-promotion.test.ts
    Tests  41 passed (41)                         # full dreaming-phases.test.ts
    ```
  - Format/lint/typecheck on changed files: all clean. (Repo-wide format reports 34 pre-existing issues on `main` unrelated to this PR.)
- Observed result after fix: the helper rejects any rehydrated range that touches a `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:start -->` or `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:*:end -->` marker line. No regressions in the existing `lineRangeOverlapsDreamingFence` describe block or the broader test files.
- What was not tested: An end-to-end repro via `applyShortTermPromotions` that drives `relocateCandidateRange` to organically pick a marker-line-bordering window. Probe attempts on current `main` could not construct an organic trigger — the relocator's compare/quality scoring rarely prefers such windows when cleaner alternatives exist in the file — so the demonstration is at the unit level. The repo-wide format/lint/build/CI suite was not run locally; the changed files pass all relevant lanes individually.

target.title: fix(gateway): rejected config.patch/apply never queues SIGUSR1 restart
target.body:
## Summary

Pins the invariant that a **rejected** `config.patch` / `config.apply` RPC never schedules a gateway SIGUSR1 restart, with both a structural guard and regression tests. Filed in response to a downstream incident where a long-running gateway ended up in a permanent "draining for restart" zombie state after two consecutive rejected `config.patch`/`config.apply` calls.

## Reproduction

Inside a long-running gateway:

1. A control-plane caller invokes `config.patch` to inject `channels.slack.*` tokens.
2. Gateway rejects: `gateway config.patch cannot change protected config paths: channels.slack.botToken, channels.slack.appToken, ...`
3. Caller retries with `config.apply` (full file). Gateway rejects again with the same protected-paths error.
4. **Despite both patches being rejected**, the reload manager logged `config change requires gateway restart (channels.slack) — deferring until N operation(s), R reply(ies), E embedded run(s) complete` and queued two SIGUSR1s ~2s apart.
5. The second SIGUSR1 arrived during the in-process restart window (after `ready` but before `starting channels and sidecars` finished). From that moment forward every model call returned `Gateway is draining for restart; new tasks are not accepted`. State persisted for ~75 minutes until recovery via process restart.

## Diagnosis

Reading the current gateway code, the reload manager already only acts on *successfully-applied* writes:
- The chokidar file-watcher path reads the on-disk snapshot via `readSnapshot()`.
- The in-process write path goes through `notifyRuntimeConfigWriteListeners`, which fires *only* after `replaceConfigFile` commits.

And `config.ts:configHandlers["config.patch"]` and `["config.apply"]` both validate (parseConfigJson5 → applyMergePatch → restoreRedactedValues → validateConfigObjectWithPlugins → ensureResolvableSecretRefsOrRespond → diffConfigPaths) **before** calling `commitGatewayConfigWrite` and `resolveGatewayConfigRestartWriteResult`. Any rejection branch calls `respond(false, ..., errorShape(...))` and returns without writing.

So the structural invariant *holds today* — but it's enforced by control flow alone, not by a structural guard, and the observed correlation between rejected patches and a queued restart was nowhere covered by regression tests. The most likely actual mechanism in the incident is a separate successful `replaceConfigFile` write racing alongside the two rejected ones, which is a legitimate restart trigger but was temporally indistinguishable in the logs from the rejected patches.

This PR locks the rejection-no-restart invariant in place so a future refactor can't break it.

## Changes

### `src/gateway/server-methods/config-write-flow.ts`

`resolveGatewayConfigRestartWriteResult` now short-circuits when `changedPaths.length === 0`:
- Refuses to write the restart sentinel.
- Refuses to call `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart`.
- Logs an audit warning with the control-plane actor.

Callers (`config.patch` and `config.apply` handlers in `config.ts`) only invoke this helper after `commitGatewayConfigWrite` succeeds and a non-empty `changedPaths` is computed, so this guard is a defense-in-depth backstop, not a behavior change in any current code path.

### `src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts`

New regression suite `gateway rejected config writes do not queue restart` spies on `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` via `vi.spyOn(restartModule, ...)` and asserts no call happens when:

- `config.patch` is rejected for invalid JSON (parse failure)
- `config.patch` is rejected for `raw=null` (non-object)
- `config.patch` is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
- `config.apply` is rejected for invalid raw (parse failure)
- `config.apply` is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
- **Two rejected patches arrive consecutively** — direct replay of the incident scenario (config.patch rejected → config.apply rejected, neither queues a restart)

Each test also asserts the on-disk config hash is unchanged across the rejection.

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] Rejected `config.patch`/`config.apply` calls do not trigger reload evaluation or restart queue → covered by 6 regression tests
- [x] Reload manager only acts on successfully-applied config diffs → already enforced (reload reads disk / subscribes to post-commit notifications); now also backed by the `changedPaths.length === 0` guard
- [x] Regression test: rejected protected-path patch → no reload, no restart, gateway state unchanged → covered (the agent-side `assertGatewayConfigMutationAllowed` protected-paths guard is already covered by `src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts`; this PR adds the server-side rejection coverage)
- [ ] **Stretch:** detect & coalesce duplicate SIGUSR1 arriving during the in-process restart window — **not included in this PR**, see Out of scope below

## Test results

```
pnpm vitest --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts
  src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts
    gateway rejected config writes do not queue restart
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.patch is rejected for invalid JSON
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.patch is rejected for non-object raw
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.patch is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.apply is rejected for invalid raw
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.apply is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when two rejected patches arrive consecutively
```

Full `vitest.gateway-server` config: **1045 passing**, 1 pre-existing failure in `gateway config methods > returns noop for config.patch when config is unchanged` (asserts `res.payload?.noop` is `true` but server returns `noop` differently; verified on `main` at the current HEAD). Not introduced by this PR.

Full `vitest.gateway-methods`: **831 passing**.  
Full `vitest.gateway-core`: **1938 passing** (includes all `config-reload.test.ts` and `server-reload-handlers.test.ts`).  
`src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts`: **26 passing × 2 projects** (existing agent-side protected-path coverage unchanged).  
`src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts`: **27 passing** (existing SIGUSR1 coalescing coverage unchanged).

## Out of scope

**Coalescing duplicate SIGUSR1 arriving during in-process restart startup window** — `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` in `src/infra/restart.ts` already coalesces against `pendingRestartTimer`/`hasUnconsumedRestartSignal`, but `run-loop.ts` resets `shuttingDown=false` when a new lifecycle iteration begins, which is what let the second SIGUSR1 from the original incident race into the new lifecycle's startup window. Fixing this requires deeper changes to the run-loop's signal acceptance window and warrants a separate PR with its own test plan.

target.title: fix(cron): add Authorization header to provider preflight probe
target.body:
## Summary

Replaced the hand-rolled Authorization header logic in the cron preflight probe with the shared `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()` from `provider-request-config.ts`. This ensures the preflight and normal model request paths build identical auth headers for the same config.

## Changes

### P1: Reuse shared auth normalization (ClawSweeper finding)

- **model-preflight.runtime.ts**: Replaced the manual `if (params.apiKey) { headers["Authorization"] = ... }` / custom-header construction with delegation to `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()` via `sanitizeConfiguredProviderRequest()`
- Auth is now resolved through the same code path as normal model requests — any future changes to auth formatting apply automatically
- Full credential chain preserved: `request.auth` override → `resolveApiKeyForProvider` fallback → no auth
- Also ensures `request.headers` are always passed through (even without `request.auth`), fixing a pre-existing gap where provider-level and request-level headers were silently dropped

### P0: Error redaction at cache-write time

- **model-preflight.runtime.ts**: Applied `formatErrorMessage()` to errors before storing in `preflightCache`, preventing unredacted error objects from remaining in heap between cache write and read

### P0: Header prefix trimming consistency

- Prefix is now trimmed via shared `sanitizeConfiguredProviderRequest`, matching the behavior of `resolveAuthOverride` in the normal request path

### P2: Cross-path auth header parity tests

- **model-preflight.runtime.test.ts**: Added 4 parity tests (P0) verifying preflight and normal path produce identical auth headers for the same config
- Added 4 edge case tests (P1): empty header fields, unknown mode, whitespace token, OAuth marker filtering
- Total: 31 tests pass

## Files Changed

| File | Δ |
|------|---|
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.ts` | +140/-40 |
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.test.ts` | +445/-3 |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Cron job preflight was building custom auth headers (`request.auth.mode === "header"`) with hand-rolled logic that missed prefix trimming, header collision cleanup, and `request.headers` merging — inconsistent with the shared auth normalization used by normal model requests.

The fix replaces the entire preflight auth resolution with delegation to `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()`, the same shared function used by the normal model request path. This ensures:
- Authorization header and custom-header auth are built identically in both paths
- Prefix trimming and header collision cleanup apply automatically
- `request.headers` are always forwarded (even without `request.auth`)

**Real environment tested:**
- **OS:** Ubuntu 24.04 (Oracle VPS)
- **Runtime:** Node.js v24.14.0
- **OpenClaw version:** 2026.5.18 (npm install)
- **Provider:** LiteLLM proxy at http://127.0.0.1:4000
- **Auth method:** LITELLM_API_KEY environment variable

**Exact steps or command run after this patch (original base auth case):**
```bash
node -e "const m = require('./dist/index.cjs'); m.probeCronModelProvider({ provider: 'litellm', model: 'gpt-4o' }).then(r => console.log(JSON.stringify(r)))"
```

**Evidence after fix:** LiteLLM proxy logs before (top) and after (bottom) the patch show GET /models now gets 200 OK instead of 401:

![Before/after terminal comparison](https://github.com/samson1357924/openclaw/releases/download/evd-pr83648/evidence-pr-img.jpg)

Before — no auth header, cron preflight triggers 401:
```
LiteLLM Proxy:ERROR - Exception occured - No api key passed in.
```

After — auth header sent, preflight succeeds:
```
127.0.0.1:45914 - "GET /models HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
```

The comprehensive auth normalization refactoring (prefix trim, header merging, parity with normal path) is covered by the test suite below.

**Additional verification (unit test suite):**
```
 ✓ src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.test.ts (31 tests) 575ms
 ✓ src/agents/provider-request-config.test.ts (30 tests) 62ms
All 61 tests pass across both files. No regressions.

```
Coverage includes:
- 4 cross-path auth header parity tests (preflight headers match normal path headers)
- 4 edge case tests (empty header fields, unknown mode, whitespace token, OAuth marker)
- Existing bearer auth, header mode, and fallback tests

**Observed result after fix:**
- Live LiteLLM probe: GET /models changed from 401 Unauthorized to 200 OK
- Unit tests: 31/31 pass (preflight), 30/30 pass (regression)
- Code review: no Critical or Important issues found across all 5 dimensions
- Auth delegation is correct with proper fallback semantics and edge case handling

**What was not tested:**
- Integration test with actual local provider endpoint (preflight tests mock `fetchWithSsrFGuard`)
- `prepareProviderRuntimeAuth` plugin auth exchange path (documented gap — preflight intentionally skips this for local provider probes)
- Provider-level headers (`providerConfig.headers`) — these require SecretInput resolution not available at preflight's raw config stage

target.title: feat(telegram): add guest mode
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: OpenClaw did not support Telegram Guest Mode, so users could not summon a Telegram bot for a one-off answer from supported chats unless the bot was already participating through normal Telegram message handling. - Why it matters: Guest Mode lets an allowed Telegram user ask the bot for a visible, one-shot answer in a external group or normal 1:1 private chats between users, without turning that chat into a normal OpenClaw conversation. - What changed: Telegram Guest Mode can now be enabled explicitly. Allowed guest requests are routed through an isolated direct-style agent turn, preserve sender metadata, inspect supported current/replied Telegram media when Telegram provides file references, preserve configured Telegram account/channel agent routing, and deliver exactly one visible text response. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal Telegram group/direct message handling remains on the existing send path. Guest Mode is disabled by default and does not inherit per-group policies or normal group conversation bindings; it uses account-level Telegram allowlist rules while preserving configured account/channel agent routing. Telegram Guest answers are text/article-only: the Bot API answerGuestQuery endpoint does not accept local file uploads, so generated or local file outputs are described rather than attached in Guest Mode. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #79077 - Partially addresses #79077 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram bots can answer Guest Bot queries from supported Telegram chats, including groups where the bot is not a member and 1:1 private chats with other users, when `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true` is configured. - Real environment tested: Ubuntu Linux, local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway, Telegram channel configured, OpenAI Codex with GPT-5.5. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Activate the guest mode in the BotFather telegram bot settings. 2. Set `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`. 3. Built OpenClaw from this branch. 4. Built Control UI assets. 5. Restarted the local gateway. 6. Sent a Telegram guest query to the bot from an (external group where the bot was not a member, or a private chat with another user). (`@openclawbot what time is it? What is my telegram id, what is this channel / group id?`) 7. Verified that one visible answer appeared in Telegram. 8. Verified that users not allowed by the account-level allowlist could not use Guest Mode. 9. Tested groups where the bot is already a member to check that normal Telegram group handling did not regress. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): - Observed result after fix: The guest query created an isolated direct-style Telegram guest session and produced one visible Telegram answer. Unauthorized guest senders were blocked by account-level allowFrom. Normal groups where the bot is already a member continued to work through the existing Telegram message path. - What was not tested: Bot-to-bot mode, long-running multi-account (more than 3) production soak testing, cross-platform release-package install proof, and cached file_id media-output delivery for Guest Mode. Direct local file upload in answerGuestQuery is not in scope because Telegram does not expose an upload parameter for this endpoint. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, OpenClaw did not include guest_message in Telegram allowed updates and had no answerGuestQuery delivery path, so Guest Bot queries were not converted into agent turns. (So this did just not exist previously) ## Root Cause (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: N/A for a new feature. Telegram Guest Bots use a new update and response shape that the existing Telegram channel did not support. - Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Telegram tests covered normal message updates, but not guest_message allowed updates, guest session isolation, or answerGuestQuery delivery. - Contributing context (if known): Guest Mode is similar to inline behavior from a product perspective, but Telegram delivers it as a guest message that must be answered through the Bot API guest-answer endpoint rather than the normal send-message path. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/allowed-updates.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/webhook.test.ts - Scenario the test should lock in: A guest update is accepted, routed to an isolated guest session, keeps sender metadata available to the agent, preserves configured Telegram account/channel agent routing, does not inherit normal group bindings or group policies, and sends only one visible Telegram guest answer. Guest updates are only requested when Guest Mode is enabled, and guest update queueing is keyed by source chat + sender instead of the normal group lane. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the Telegram plugin boundary where allowed updates, bot capability detection, guest routing, session context, authorization, and delivery behavior meet, without requiring live Telegram credentials. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A. New tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Telegram Guest Mode can now be enabled with `channels.telegram.guest.enabled`. - omitted / `false`: ignore guest queries and do not request `guest_message` updates from Telegram. - `true`: request and handle Telegram guest queries. Guest Mode uses the Telegram account-level allowlist. It intentionally does not apply per-group allowlists or normal group conversation bindings because the bot is not a member of the source group in the guest interaction. Guest Mode can inspect supported media included in the guest message or replied message when Telegram file references are available, but generated/local media outputs are not uploaded through answerGuestQuery; the agent should explain that limitation in the one visible text answer. ## Diagram (if applicable) For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`. ```text Before: [Telegram guest_message] -> [not in allowed updates / no guest delivery path] -> [no visible answer] After: [Telegram guest_message] -> [account-level allowlist] -> [isolated direct-style guest session] -> [agent turn] -> [answerGuestQuery] -> [one visible Telegram answer] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) Yes. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No. - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) Yes. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No. - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This adds a Telegram guest-response capability using Telegram Bot API `answerGuestQuery`. Guest access is gated by the existing Telegram account-level `allowFrom` list, and OpenClaw only requests `guest_message` updates when Guest Mode is explicitly enabled. Guest turns use isolated direct-style session keys, preserve configured Telegram account/channel agent routing, skip group conversation bindings, do not receive source chat history, and deliver only one visible response. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Ubuntu Linux. - Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway. - Model/provider: OpenAI Codex, GPT-5.5. - Integration/channel (if any): Telegram. - Relevant config (redacted): Telegram channel configured with `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`; bot token and account details redacted. ### Steps 1. Enable guest mode for the bot using the telegram BotFather. 2. Configure `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`. 3. Build OpenClaw from this branch. 4. Build Control UI assets. 5. Restart the local gateway. 6. Send a Telegram guest query to the bot from an external group where the bot is not a member (or a private chat with another user). 7. Confirm that Telegram shows one visible bot answer. 8. Send a guest query from a user that is not allowed by account-level allowFrom. 9. Send normal messages in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Expected - Allowed guest queries create an isolated guest session and produce one visible Telegram answer. - Unauthorized guest senders are blocked. - Normal Telegram group handling still works in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Actual - Allowed guest queries produced one visible answer. - Unauthorized guest senders were blocked. - Existing normal Telegram group handling still worked. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [X] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Live Telegram Guest Mode round trip from a group where the bot was not a member. - Normal Telegram private chats with the bot. - Normal Telegram private chats with other users using the Guest Mode work. - Unauthorized guest sender blocked by account-level allowlist. - Normal Telegram groups where the bot is already a member still handled messages normally. - Control UI assets load after pnpm ui:build. - Local retest after the account-bound guest routing fix. - Edge cases checked: - Unauthorized guest sender fallback. - Guest isolation from normal group bindings. (Needs to remove bot in between from the group) - What you did not verify: - Bot-to-bot mode. - Long-running multi-account (>3) production soak. - Cross-platform release-package install proof. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. Local Codex review was run during the beta-branch iteration and again after moving the patch onto current `origin/main`. Actionable findings from the earlier rounds were addressed before opening the PR. The final main-based Codex review reported no discrete actionable correctness issues. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes. - Config/env changes? (Yes/No) Yes. - Migration needed? (Yes/No) No. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Telegram Guest Mode supports only one visible answer for this interaction shape. - Mitigation: Guest delivery sends only the first final answer through answerGuestQuery. - Risk: Unauthorized users could trigger guest turns (new ingress style). - Mitigation: Guest authorization uses the Telegram account-level allowFrom list, matching the Telegram DM access boundary. ~Horo

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

## Change Type
- [x] Feature

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the policy is applied to the spawned child embedded runtime so the child receives only allowed tool schemas. Gateway `agent.run` also normalizes direct `toolsAllow` input before runner dispatch.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout at current PR head `230b54baa3175fafeb7160922f510b489a6d1887`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, local Gateway on loopback token auth with `gateway.mode=local`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json` through Gateway (not `--local`), and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server that records every `/v1/responses` request tool schema.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a local mock OpenAI Responses server from `/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/mock-openai-toolsallow.cjs`, started OpenClaw Gateway from the same checkout, then ran the parent agent through Gateway:

```text
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=<loopback-proof-token> OPENAI_API_KEY=mock node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json --session-key agent:default:tools-allow-parent --message 'SUBAGENT_TOOLS_ALLOW_PARENT_PROOF: call sessions_spawn exactly once with toolsAllow ["read"].' --timeout 90
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head Gateway proof summarizer. The parent initial request had `sessions_spawn`; the spawned child request had only `read`, with `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, and `sessions_yield` absent. The mock log had 3 requests total: parent initial tool call, parent follow-up after child result, and the restricted spawned child request.

```json
{
  "head": "230b54baa3175fafeb7160922f510b489a6d1887",
  "gatewayAgentRunStatus": "ok",
  "gatewayAgentRunId": "8843fa2b-8a41-49d6-aaa4-ae3487155aa5",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "CHILD_DONE",
  "parentTools": [
    "exec",
    "read",
    "sessions_spawn",
    "sessions_yield",
    "subagents"
  ],
  "restrictedChildTools": [
    "read"
  ],
  "restrictedChildHasRead": true,
  "restrictedChildHasExec": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsSpawn": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsYield": false,
  "totalMockRequests": 3,
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs"
}
```

Additional current-head verification already run after resolving conflicts:

```text
node scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check
prompt snapshot check passed

node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8
oxlint shards passed

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --testNamePattern 'toolsAllow|timestamp context|canonical Anthropic' --reporter=dot
focused toolsAllow vitest shards passed
```

GitHub checks observed on current head: PR is mergeable/clean and the current `Real behavior proof` workflow check is passing.

Observed result after fix: A real Gateway-backed parent agent run called `sessions_spawn` with `toolsAllow:["read"]`, the child run completed with `CHILD_DONE`, and the actual spawned child model request only advertised `read`. Disallowed parent/session/runtime tools were not present in the spawned child request.

What was not tested: A paid external provider was not used. The proof used a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server to avoid provider cost while exercising the real Gateway, real embedded runner, real `sessions_spawn` tool call, and real child model request construction.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] Prompt snapshot check
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`, `src/commands/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`, `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*`.
- Scenario locked in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects unsupported ACP runtime, forwards through Gateway/session state, reaches embedded runs started by the command runner, is inherited by persisted child sessions, and is forwarded to CLI harnesses for fail-closed handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow a spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects `toolsAllow` clearly because ACP cannot enforce this embedded-runner allowlist.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. Unsupported ACP paths reject the option so callers do not get a false sense that an allowlist was enforced. CLI-backed runs receive the runtime allowlist at the harness boundary so the harness can fail closed when enforcement is unavailable.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect all CLI harnesses to enforce runtime allowlists.
  - Mitigation: OpenClaw forwards the allowlist to the CLI harness boundary for fail-closed handling instead of silently dropping it.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native child tools with `toolsAllow`; the spawned child embedded model request advertises only the allowed tools instead of inheriting the full parent native tool set.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `99c0bdd3cb556a7a407c0ae45e0931fa82617dc3`; temporary OpenClaw state/config; real Gateway loopback token auth; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json`; deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server logging `/v1/responses` requests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a repo-local Node proof harness that created temp state/config, started the mock OpenAI-compatible server, started the real Gateway, then invoked `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json --session-key agent:default:tools-allow-parent --message 'SUBAGENT_TOOLS_ALLOW_PARENT_PROOF: call sessions_spawn exactly once with toolsAllow ["read"].' --timeout 120` with the temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "head": "99c0bdd3cb556a7a407c0ae45e0931fa82617dc3",
  "gatewayAgentExitCode": 0,
  "gatewayAgentRunStatus": "ok",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "CHILD_DONE",
  "stopReason": "stop",
  "toolSummary": {
    "calls": 1,
    "tools": ["sessions_spawn"],
    "failures": 0
  },
  "parentTools": ["agents_list", "apply_patch", "browser", "canvas", "create_goal", "cron", "dir_fetch", "dir_list", "edit", "exec", "file_fetch", "file_write", "gateway", "get_goal", "image", "image_generate", "memory_get", "memory_search", "message", "music_generate", "nodes", "pdf", "process", "qqbot_remind", "read", "session_status", "sessions_history", "sessions_list", "sessions_send", "sessions_spawn", "sessions_yield", "skill_workshop", "subagents", "tts", "update_goal", "update_plan", "video_generate", "web_fetch", "web_search", "write"],
  "restrictedChildTools": ["read"],
  "restrictedChildHasRead": true,
  "restrictedChildHasExec": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsSpawn": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsYield": false,
  "totalMockRequests": 3,
  "proofDir": "/var/folders/b_/fvf7_fqd4y77y63pd76x8gb80000gn/T/openclaw-78441-proof-head-99c0bdd3-J7y7zd"
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The parent request still exposes the full native tool set and calls `sessions_spawn` once, while the spawned child request exposes only `["read"]`; it has `read` and does not have `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, or `sessions_yield`. The real Gateway agent run exits 0 with status `ok`.
- What was not tested: A paid external provider. The proof used a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock to avoid cost while still exercising the real Gateway, embedded runner, `sessions_spawn`, and child model request construction.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds an operation-aware outbound delivery resolver, routes text, poll, direct delivery, and gateway poll paths through it, and drains QQBot pending deliveries on ready/resumed reconnect callbacks.

PR surface: Source +147, Tests +650. Total +797 across 12 files.

target.title: fix: skip setup-only channel plugins in outbound resolution and drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect
target.body:
## Summary

When the qqbot WebSocket reconnects after a session timeout (close code `4009`), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returns a setup-only plugin stub that lacks any send capability. This causes the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error, and all messages queued during the disconnection window are permanently lost.

## Root Cause

`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolves the first matching plugin entry for a channel id without verifying that the entry actually has send capability (`outbound.sendText`, `message.send.text`, etc.). During qqbot WebSocket reconnection, a setup-only plugin entry (registered for configuration/pairing but not for message delivery) shadows the real sending plugin, causing the resolution to short-circuit with a non-functional stub.

Regression introduced in 2026.5.28 (commit `e932160`).

## Fix

### 1. Split outbound channel resolution into shared and delivery paths (`channel-resolution.ts`)

Add a `channelPluginCanSend()` predicate that checks for any of the 10 known send methods across both the legacy `outbound` adapter and the newer `message.send` adapter:

- `outbound.sendText / sendMedia / sendPayload / sendPoll / sendFormattedText / sendFormattedMedia`
- `message.send.text / media / payload / poll`

Instead of gating the shared `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolver (which would break action-only channel plugins that have `actions.handleAction` but no send capability), a new **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`** resolver applies the send-capability guard exclusively for message delivery paths.

- **`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()`**: Shared resolver — returns any matching plugin regardless of send capability, preserving resolution for action dispatch, target resolution, and other non-send callers.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`**: Delivery resolver — skips setup-only entries and only returns plugins that can actually send messages.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelMessageAdapter()`**: Updated to use the delivery resolver.
- **`message.ts`**: `sendMessage`/`sendPoll` updated to use the delivery resolver.

### 2. Align the direct delivery path with the operation-aware resolver (`deliver.ts`)

The preflight resolver was operation-aware, but the actual direct delivery handler reloaded the adapter capability-blind: `createChannelHandler` → `loadBootstrappedOutboundAdapter` → `loadChannelOutboundAdapter` resolved the first registry entry for the channel id (`createChannelRegistryLoader((entry) => entry.plugin.outbound)`) without checking send capability. A setup-only shell with a defined-but-non-send `outbound` (`{ deliveryMode: "direct" }`, no `sendText`) pinned ahead of the runtime sender therefore still shadowed the real adapter on the direct send path, throwing `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` even after preflight had selected a valid plugin. This also affected the reconnect drain path, which has no preflight.

`loadBootstrappedOutboundAdapter` now resolves through the same `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()` resolver as preflight (with `allowBootstrap`, operation `text`), so preflight and the actual direct delivery handler share one send-capability-aware resolution path. The previous manual bootstrap-and-retry is removed because the resolver's `allowBootstrap` performs the equivalent lazy materialize-then-retry. The generic registry loader and the CLI/SDK surfaces are left untouched.

### 3. Drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect (`extensions/qqbot/src/channel.ts`)

The `onReady` and `onResumed` gateway callbacks now call `drainPendingDeliveries()` from the plugin SDK delivery queue runtime, recovering messages that were queued while the WebSocket was disconnected. The drain is:

- Filtered by channel (`qqbot`) and account id, with a fallback for default-account entries where `accountId` may be `undefined`
- Deduplicated via `drainKey` to prevent concurrent drains
- Wrapped in try/catch so drain failures do not break the reconnection flow

### 4. Route the gateway poll handler through the operation-aware resolver (`src/gateway/server-methods/send.ts`)

The gateway `poll` handler still resolved its outbound plugin via the operation-blind `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()`, while `sendPoll` (`message.ts`) and the direct text delivery path (`deliver.ts`) already routed through the operation-aware `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`. This left the gateway poll endpoint as the last surface where a setup-only / text-only first-match plugin could shadow a poll-capable runtime fallback and force a spurious `"unsupported poll channel"`, even though the new resolver test proves the poll-capable fallback should win.

The poll handler now resolves through `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery({ operation: "poll", allowBootstrap: true })`, mirroring the text delivery path. The `durationSeconds` / `isAnonymous` capability checks below the resolution now read from the same poll-capable plugin that serves `sendPoll`, so the capability gate and the actual send no longer disagree. The two non-poll resolver call sites in the same file (the `send` and `action` surfaces) are intentionally left on the shared resolver, since action-only plugins must still resolve without send capability.

## Tests

- `channel-resolution.test.ts`: setup-only plugin returns from shared resolver, action-only plugin resolves from shared but not delivery resolver, delivery resolver skips setup-only and returns sending fallback, legacy outbound adapter, poll-only outbound
- `deliver.test.ts`: new unmocked regression driving the real `deliverOutboundPayloads` → `createChannelHandler` → `loadBootstrappedOutboundAdapter` path with a pinned setup-only shell that exposes a defined non-send `outbound` plus an active send-capable fallback, asserting the send-capable adapter actually delivers (fails before the Fix 2 alignment with `"Outbound not configured"`, passes after)
- `channel.test.ts`: onReady drain, onResumed drain

Fixes #88955.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After a QQ Bot WebSocket disconnect (session takeover / close code 4009), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returned a setup-only plugin stub instead of the runtime entry, causing `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` and dropping all pending deliveries.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw `2026.6.2` (commit `b90683e`, branch `fix/qqbot-reconnect-outbound-88955`), macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. QQ Bot plugin `@openclaw/qqbot@2026.5.31-beta.4` with live QQ Bot credentials (appId `<redacted>`).

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Built patched OpenClaw from fix branch: `pnpm build && npm pack --ignore-scripts`
  2. Installed globally: `npm install -g openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz` — confirmed version: `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)`
  3. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  4. Observed qqbot plugin startup, WebSocket connection, and `Gateway ready` callback sequence in logs
  5. Verified normal QQ Bot C2C message round-trip: user sent "hello" → bot received → processed → replied with markdown (20 chars, 200 OK)
  6. Scanned full gateway log for `"Outbound not configured"` errors — zero matches
  7. Repeated restart cycle to confirm reconnect stability across multiple `Gateway ready` events

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  # Patched OpenClaw installed and running
  $ openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)

  # Gateway restart sequence — qqbot reconnects cleanly
  2026-06-02T22:38:53.727+08:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; restarting
  2026-06-02T22:39:15.986+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.6s)
  2026-06-02T22:39:16.171+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Starting gateway — appId=<redacted>, enabled=true
  2026-06-02T22:39:17.387+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Connecting to wss://api.sgroup.qq.com/websocket
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.468+08:00 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.641+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready

  # Successful QQ Bot message delivery after reconnect
  2026-06-02T22:32:40.229+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Processing message from <redacted>: hello
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.431+08:00 [qqbot] [default] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.432+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Sent markdown chunk (20/20 chars) with 0 HTTP images (c2c)

  # Zero outbound resolution errors across entire log history
  $ grep -c "Outbound not configured.*qqbot" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log
  0

  # Multiple Gateway ready events over 4+ hours — all clean reconnects
  $ grep "qqbot.*Gateway ready" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log | wc -l
  18
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The delivery resolver correctly skipped the setup-only plugin entry (which has no `sendText`/`sendMedia` methods) and resolved the runtime entry with full send capability. Outbound messages delivered successfully through the QQ Bot API (HTTP 200). Zero occurrences of the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error across 18 reconnect cycles over a 4+ hour observation window. The shared resolver continued to return all plugin entries for non-send callers (action dispatch, target resolution), preserving action-only plugin compatibility.

- **Direct delivery path alignment (follow-up)**: A reviewer noted that the preflight fix did not cover the actual direct delivery handler construction, which reloaded the adapter capability-blind and could still let a pinned setup-only shell shadow the runtime sender. `loadBootstrappedOutboundAdapter` now routes through the same `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()` resolver as preflight. Verified locally on the fix branch (macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0): the new unmocked `deliver.test.ts` regression fails before the alignment (`Error: Outbound not configured for channel: matrix` thrown from `createChannelHandler`) and passes after; full focused suite green (`channel-resolution`, `message`, `message.channels`, `deliver`, qqbot `channel` — 169 tests passing), `pnpm tsgo:prod` and `pnpm tsgo:test` clean, `pnpm build` clean, `git diff --check` clean, lint clean.

- **Gateway poll resolver alignment (follow-up)**: A reviewer noted the gateway poll endpoint (`src/gateway/server-methods/send.ts`) still used the operation-blind `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()`, so a setup-only / text-only first match could shadow a poll-capable fallback and return `"unsupported poll channel"` despite a working poll plugin existing. The poll handler now routes through `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery({ operation: "poll", allowBootstrap: true })`, matching `sendPoll` and the direct text delivery path. Verified locally on the fix branch (macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0): a new gateway `send.test.ts` regression where the first-match plugin is a text-only shell (no `sendPoll`) and a poll-capable plugin is registered as the runtime fallback — the handler now skips the shell and dispatches the poll via the fallback (`messageId: poll-fallback-1`) instead of returning `"unsupported poll channel"`; the test fails under the old operation-blind resolver and passes after the change. Full gateway send suite green (`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts` — 124 tests passing), `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` clean. The two non-poll resolver call sites in the same file are intentionally untouched.

- **What was not tested**:

  Live drain proof is not reproducible on the fixed build. The `drainReconnectPendingDeliveries()` mechanism (Fix 2) was verified via unit tests but not observed draining actual queued entries in a live environment. This is not an oversight — it is a structural consequence of the resolver fix (Fix 1) eliminating the only deterministic cause of pending qqbot entries. Code-level analysis confirms this:

  **How the delivery queue works** (`deliver.ts:1254-1280`): Every outbound delivery is write-ahead enqueued via `enqueueDelivery()` before any send attempt. On success, `ackDelivery()` removes the entry (`deliver.ts:1367`). On throw, `failDelivery()` bumps `retryCount` and **leaves it pending** (`delivery-queue-storage.ts:137-144`).

  **What created the pending entries in #88955**: The `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` throw fired from `createChannelHandler` (`deliver.ts:223-231`) when `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery` returned a setup-only plugin with no send methods. This throw occurs **outside** the per-payload try/catch (`deliver.ts:1451`), so it propagated to the queue-cleanup catch → `failDelivery` → entry left pending. The resolver fix eliminates this throw by filtering setup-only entries via `channelPluginCanSend()`.

  **Why transient QQ API failures also do not create pending entries**: The qqbot engine **swallows** HTTP/network errors into returned `{error}` result objects instead of throwing (`outbound.ts:301-304, 313-318`). The delivery core text branch records "sent" purely on `deliveredResults.length > 0` with no message-id identity gate (`deliver.ts:1772-1778`), so a swallowed engine error yields a "sent" outcome → `ackDelivery` → entry removed, not pending.

  **The drain's real value**: It is a belt-and-suspenders safeguard for the **upgrade path** — entries left pending (with `lastError: "Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`) by an unfixed build are drained immediately after a fixed gateway reconnects. The `bypassBackoff` predicate explicitly matches this exact error string (`/outbound not configured/i`), ensuring these entries skip retry backoff and are retried on the first reconnect event. If the retry still fails (e.g., the plugin is genuinely misconfigured), `isPermanentDeliveryError` (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:79`) moves the entry to `failed/` — no infinite retry loop. `MAX_RETRIES` is also enforced before the bypass branch (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:544`). The drain also covers the narrow crash-in-enqueue-to-ack window handled by `recoverPendingDeliveries` at startup (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`). The drain selection filter (`channel === "qqbot"` + account id match + bypassBackoff for `"Outbound not configured"`, `channel.test.ts:90-152`) and the reconnect-drain integration logic (`delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts`) are exercised by 35 unit tests (all passing).

  **Seeded pending-drain proof**: To validate the drain recovery path end-to-end, a pending delivery entry was manually seeded into the SQLite delivery queue to simulate the upgrade scenario (entries left by an unfixed build):

  1. Inserted a pending entry: `id=seeded-drain-test-001`, `channel=qqbot`, `accountId=default`, `lastError="Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`, `retry_count=1`
  2. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Observed recovery sequence in logs:

  ```
  00:30:00.353 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.9s)
  00:30:00.987 [delivery-recovery] Found 1 pending delivery entries — starting recovery
  00:30:01.567 [qqbot] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Recovered delivery seeded-drain-test-001 on qqbot
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Delivery recovery complete: 1 recovered, 0 failed, 0 skipped (max retries), 0 deferred (backoff)
  00:30:02.816 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  00:30:02.952 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready
  ```

  4. Verified entry removed from DB: `SELECT count(*) FROM delivery_queue_entries WHERE channel='qqbot' AND status='pending'` → **0**
  5. Zero `"Outbound not configured"` errors after restart

  **Key observation**: The `0 deferred (backoff)` in the recovery summary confirms the `bypassBackoff` predicate (`/outbound not configured/i`) correctly matched the seeded entry's `lastError`, allowing immediate retry without waiting for backoff. The entry was successfully delivered (HTTP 200) and acked (removed from queue).

  **Note**: The startup `recoverPendingDeliveries` path (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`) executed before the qqbot `onReady` callback (`00:30:00.987` vs `00:30:02.952`), so startup recovery handled the entry before the reconnect drain fired. Both paths share the same underlying drain logic and bypass predicates; the startup path simply runs first. A naturally-occurring live drain on the `onReady` path cannot be reliably reproduced because the fix removes the deterministic failure that created those entries, and startup recovery pre-empts the reconnect drain for entries present at boot. Multi-shard configurations were not tested.

target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ## Scenario coverage | Scenario | Expected behavior | | --- | --- | | Default Codex app-server run with non-empty `SOUL.md` | Stable profile files are current-turn context; Codex native personality is disabled; bridge points personality at `SOUL.md`. | | Default Codex app-server run with empty `SOUL.md` | Empty placeholder content does not suppress Codex's native personality overlay. | | User edits `SOUL.md` mid-session in default mode | Next turn sees the edit because the file remains per-turn context. | | User opts into `thread_developer` | Stable profile files move to developer instructions; `MEMORY.md` and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain turn context. | | User edits `SOUL.md` in `thread_developer` mode | OpenClaw fingerprints the tracked developer files and starts a fresh native Codex thread. | | User edits `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` | OpenClaw fingerprints loaded native project-doc content and rotates the native Codex binding. | | Lightweight cron Codex run | OpenClaw still skips extra bootstrap prompt text for exact command preservation, does not disable native Codex project docs, and does not send `personality: "none"` unless the user explicitly configured `personalityMode: "none"`. | | Existing stale binding from the previous PR | Missing prompt-surface metadata forces a new native Codex thread, so old bindings do not preserve stale developer/user context. | | Manual `/codex resume ` binding | The minimal manual binding resumes once, then persists the current prompt-surface/personality metadata after successful resume. | ## Implementation notes - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts` adds the new config enums and resolvers. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` selects the workspace prompt surface, computes the workspace fingerprint, chooses the positive SOUL bridge behavior, and keeps native project docs out of OpenClaw turn text. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts` carries `personality`, `workspacePromptSurface`, and `workspacePromptFingerprint` through start/resume params and binding invalidation. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.ts` persists the prompt-surface metadata. - Docs now describe the architecture in `docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`, and `docs/concepts/system-prompt.md`. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Codex workspace prompt routing is selectable, defaults to fresh per-turn profile context, rotates stale native Codex bindings when native project docs or thread-developer profile files change, and only disables Codex's native personality when non-empty SOUL context is actually delivered or the user explicitly forces `personalityMode: "none"`. - Current narrowed head tested: `ad037cc543` on branch `codex/workspace-context-routing-v2`, rebased on `upstream/main` `a13468320c`. - Scope note: this head removes the prior fs-safe default flip, archive fallback, Windows ACL policy change, and dependency expectation cleanup from the PR. The only non-Codex file left is `extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts`, a behavior-preserving object-spread lint cleanup required because the repo's fail-safe changed gate lints the extension surface on this branch. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`, using a temporary OpenClaw gateway and Codex app-server model route. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` - Evidence after fix: ```text [gateway-codex-live] project-doc-budget-binding-proof { "defaultHiddenEditKeptThread": true, "hiddenEditKeptThread": true, "visibleEditRotatedThread": true, "defaultFirst": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "defaultHidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "first": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "hidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "visible": { "threadId": "019e45f3-6951-7eb0-83ab-5b009ececa6f", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "1c20f3c6e3ba646eb74b03e4b75a176df0b1494bc095406685c7e6be74275508", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" } } Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 1 passed | 2 skipped (3) Duration 94.13s ``` - Observed result after fix: With Codex's implicit native 32 KiB default, editing only bytes after byte 32,768 kept the same OpenClaw Codex binding/thread/fingerprint. With `project_doc_max_bytes = 8`, editing only bytes outside the explicit native budget kept the same binding/thread/fingerprint, while editing bytes inside the native budget rotated both the native thread and workspace prompt fingerprint. This proves OpenClaw's binding invalidation now matches Codex's native project-doc visibility on the current narrowed head for both default and explicit project-doc budgets. - What was not tested: Broad cross-platform and full-matrix coverage are left to GitHub Actions; the live proof used local macOS Codex app-server auth/model access. ## Validation Current-head focused validation from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "project_doc_max_bytes|default project-doc budget" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 3 tests passed, 195 skipped. Current-head live Codex harness proof: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 1 test passed, 2 skipped. Current-head formatting/check validation: ```bash pnpm exec oxfmt --check \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts git diff --check ``` Result: passed. Changed-gate validation: ```bash node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs -- extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. `pnpm check:changed` completed typecheck, lint, dependency/package guards, duplicate coverage, runtime sidecar loader guard, conflict-marker/changelog checks, and runtime import-cycle checks. ## Maintainer review notes This PR intentionally keeps the Codex-native lane narrow: - It does not change harness selection policy, model routing, auth routing, or Codex app-server transport behavior. - It preserves native Codex base/model instructions and native project-doc loading instead of replacing them with OpenClaw prompt text. - It keeps quality-first per-turn profile context as the default and makes token-efficient thread developer instructions opt-in. - It rotates native Codex bindings when the selected prompt surface, personality policy, visible native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. - It leaves volatile `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skill context in the per-turn lane even when stable soul/profile files are opted into thread developer instructions.
